boardgame: Mobile Suit Z Gundam game: Take off MkII
description:
This boardgame is designed based on the famous Japanese animation - The Z Gundam series. This is the first set of the Z Gundam boardgame by Bandai. Therefore the game does not contain any Z Gundam miniature.

The game comes with 12 Gundam miniatures (3 of each of the following: Gundam MkII, Rick Dias, Hi-Zaku, GM2), a game board showing the space around the Earth and Moon, a set of event cards and counters.

Players can choose to play as either Titans or AEUG. Each player has a fleet and 3 MS robots at the beginning. The player who has finished his secret mission, or gets rid of all his enemies wins the game.

The Object of the Game is to beat your opponent(s) by doing one of two things: destroy his (their) home base(s) by depleting his (their) card library or by winning and claiming five different battle cards.

Interesting sci-fi card game, with seven types of cards: Character, Weapon, Vehicle, Familiar, Action, Battle and Energy. Shadow Raven CCG (combat card game) was designed, I believe, as a true CCG. However only 99 card 'starter' decks were produced. The game is likely to remain non-collectible. Each player needs a deck of his/her own.

In the not-too distant future, a group of underground mad surgeons assemble creatures from spare mutated body parts and make them fight. Join the insanity and send your own personal monstrosity into battle against your opponent's creation.

Monstrosity is played in two phases, with two distinct decks (50+ cards each). During the first round, players acquire spare body parts (arms, legs, torsos and heads) from the black market and construct creatures in their operating theaters. As each player collects mutated limbs and tries to assemble them, their opponent attempts to steal parts, sabotage the supplies, and generally dismantle the opposing creation. Round two pits the creatures against one another, as their owners play attack and defend cards to bolster their monstrosity's fighting abilities. It's a fast-paced, gruesome, and strategic battle game best suited for two players (although up to four can play).

In this nerve wracking game, collect stocks, build your companies and get ready to look for cheaper property because the rabble is moving in.

The Slums is a 2 to 6 player game (Ages 13 and up) guaranteed to keep you sweating and wondering, "Who's going to move in next door?"

In the Slums, you will build neighborhoods centered around massive Super Stores and collect Super Store stocks. You and your opponents will devaluate the neighborhoods by building slums and destroying property.  When your stock prices fall because of slums in previously trendy neighborhoods, you must decide whether it is time to sell stock or rebuild the neighborhood. You also have the option of just buying the cheap stock and letting someone else rebuild for you. One of the unique features of the game is that you will not know who is the winner until the very last piece is played. After all, a critically placed Slum can make the difference between thousands and millions of dollars.

Game play mainly falls into three parts. The first half hour is pretty much an all out war to build up as many companies as possible and collect stock. In the middle portion of the game, players hold onto their stock waiting for opportunities to build key buildings in order to tip the tide of the game in their favor. The last portion of the game is a fast paced, white knuckled race to either destroying the biggest company on the board or playing the last piece to end the game.

Using silly words and a little brain power, try to get your friends to make a mistake in this quick playing, and highly entertaining nonsensical word game.  There are multiple levels of play. Try using only the easy words and passing the cards, or use the full rule set for plenty of mind bending fun for the whole family.

DON: CCE is a new collectible card game that allows two players to assume the roles of the head of a Criminal Organization vying for control of a major city. The Player Don uses a wide variety of Associates and Family Members to attempt to bankrupt his opponent. In addition to henchmen, a Don may play actions directly from his hand to affect either his own Associates or his opponent’s. The Don who bankrupts his opponent’s funds is the winner.

DON: CCE is set in a timeless world of crime, containing elements from the Prohibition era through the rise of the modern mob. This fantasy world of the Mafia is simulated to allow players to battle each other in a spirit reflective of classic Gangster movies.

Player Dons use their tradable cards to generate funds, control vices and even whack their opponent’s Associates. Game rules are provided to play Sealed Deck, Double Sealed Deck and Constructed decks for use in tournament play, multiple player, and direct head to head competition. The Capo starter deck is available now.  The Capo booster packs, available in Summer 2003, will add a wealth of characters, actions, and possibilities to any game of DON: CCE. The complete card set at present contains over 200 separate cards representing everything from a lowly Babbo Crew to the powerful Made Men and the infamous Penitent Wife™.  The artwork is highly stylized to represent the classic gangster photos of yesteryear with hints of contemporary crime.

Using a revolutionary new game system, Dons are able to control cards in ways that other card games have never imagined. Each player’s actions control how many cards he wishes to deploy as well as how many funds he chooses to spend.  In DON: CCE, each Don controls his fate through strategy, not luck.

Future expansions will add even more stylized elements of the genre, including more Law Enforcement, additional Associates, more Family members, and even more Vices for Dons to expand their “businesses”.

You are a journalist investigating the disappearance of a college co-ed. Each turn the player chooses from a list 
of places to investigate and then reads a paragraph to see what happens. These may involve dice checks and re-directions to yet other paragraphs. In addition there is a Time Chart -- the character must eat and sleep -- as this is also a "race against time." Based on the H.P. Lovecraft's Cthulhu Mythos.

Most players will fail several times before managing to get to the key places at the right times and win.

Published in Fantasy Gamer #3, Dec/Jan 1984

Game Idea: 
The little craftsmen in the enchanted wood are astonished: Their blocks disappear! Magician Grindlebur no doubt is involved in that. How on earth will they be able to finish the enchanted castle? The only hope now is nimble fingers and a bit of luck...

A game for 2-7 little master builders Ages 6 and up. With variant for younger players.

Bigfoot, the legendary larger-than-life creature, is on the loose! You have three men in pursuit of Bigfoot who is standing on top of his mountain domain. You're curious to learn more about Bigfoot and hope to befriend him, but he feels threatened by your presence. Will you escape as he kicks the boulder in your path or will you be forced to escape to a safe zone! Only one player will succeed in getting three men on the mountain to become Bigfoot's friend for life.

For 2-4 players, Ages 7 and up

Description from the box

Bigfoot is on the loose!

You have three men in hot pursuit of this legendary larger than life creature who stands among the trees on top of his mountain home.

You're curious to learn more about this tall man-like creature - but the normally friendly bigfoot is worried about the twelve running men who are following his giant foot prints around the mountain.

Will you escape as Bigfoot kicks the boulder down the rugged mountain-side or will you be knocked over and be forced to return to one of the four safe areas?

Only one player will succeed in getting three men to the mountain top.  Will you win the race and become Bigfoot's friend for life?

The virtual pub-crawl boardgame comes in a convenient, laminated A3 format * including rules of play and scorecards.

The object of the game is to visit all 7 pubs on the board without consuming more than 70 units of alcohol or spending more than £50 ( or the equivalent - pennies, monopoly money or even matchsticks will suffice )

Food outlets and feature squares enable players to reduce their units and add to their dwindling money reserves - there's even a betting shop for those who fancy their chances and a park for chilling out.

Although the luck of the dice is important, a fair bit of skill and judgment comes into the gameplay strategy.

The game takes about 20 - 30 minutes to complete and is readily understandable for ages 8 upwards. In theory any number of people can play but 2-6 players is ideal.


     The game can be customized to any town or area - send us details of your seven favorite pubs, take-aways etc. and we will make you a personalized version.

The time is late 20th century.  The kings of the royal family, including those of the Canary House, are very fond of melon.  But so are the queen, princess, and everyone else who works for the house such as chef, housemaid, vicar, and jester!  So a war over a piece of melon starts every day in the house, as well as among the royal houses.  Who on earth will be lucky enough to get a melon in the end?

Another Japanese import.  There are character cards, four event cards, and point cards.  The box says it's an homage to Alex Randolph.

The Great Father-of-All was worried.  An argument occurred in the Heavens about which God should be given the newly created world.  So he decided to make the Four Pillars of God compete for sole power of the world.  it would be the God who could use the power from the secret Mighty Jar most suitably, and expand his territory most widely.  It is said the world was completed in seven days.

Imported from Japan, this abstract promises to be interesting.

"Abstract" wargame. Be the ruler of the most of 22 territories. You have 5 knights and 9 pages for fighting and building castles and 1 trader for building trading posts. Each round you´ll get 10 action points for several actions At the end of the game (after 20 rounds) you´ll get 20 influence points for each castle and 10 influence points for each trading post and a unknown number of influence points which you accumulated during the 20 rounds. Only the player with the highest sum of IP controls that territory. Fighting is without dice and there are only 4 pages of rules.

Sticking the IRS does not require pins or needles or knowledge of the Internal Revenue Code. It requires tax players who are willing to take a chance with a "Tax Shelter" that may give financial security or may backfire. After four tours around the Board, the Tax player who has the most after-tax cash wins the game and has successfully been able to STICK THE IRS. The players move around the colorful board, landing on opportunities to earn income, spend money on non-deductible expenses and to purchase a variety of tax shelters. In addition, the players have a chance to receive "annual reports" that determine whether the tax shelter investment made them rich or, as in most "real life" shelters, turn out to be a bust. Finally, an exposure to the horrors and humor of audit lurks with every roll of the dice. Drilling for oil in Hawaii, establishing a baseball team in Alaska, reviving an old motion picture, and building ski condominiums in Texas are just a few of the fun and exciting tax shelter opportunities that the player is offered in STICK THE IRS. Although humorous and in good taste, the game also offers certain educational benefits in highlighting the good and the bad in the use of shelters to reduce taxes. The player who manages to best use his income, his CPA and his shelters, and therefore who manages to pay the least tax, is the winner, having successfully been able to STICK THE IRS.

Object of the game is to travel the newly implemented Interstate Highway system based on a randomly drawn Trip Card (ex. Seattle to Miami) and be the player with the most money at the conclusion of the game. 
Along the way, players can be forced to pay fees to other players by stopping at the Motels, Filling Stations, Restaurants and Repair Shops. They can also be fined for speeding along their selected route. 
Conclusion of the game occurs when the first player reaches his destination (receiving a $50) Bonus. Monies are totaled and the player with the most money wins.

boardgame: White Glove Girl: A Manpower Game
description:
Obviously a promotional/motivational item from the Manpower Employment Agency. 
The object of the game is to be the first player to attain all 4 goals: Children's College Education ($2,000) - Vacation-Travel ($600) - Remodel Home ($750) - New Wardrobe ($200).
The goals are attained by moving 4 colored tokens around the board from START to HOME and then paying for the goal. 
The first player to move all 4 of her tokens to the HOME spaces is declared the WINNER.

On an hypothetical American territory, the European colonizing nations of the XVII century send their expeditions searching for buried treasures. At the same time they try to hinder and eliminate the opponents, thanks to the feeling of hate separating the Indios and the white devils.

There are two ways to win the game: find half of the hidden treasures with your Conquistadors, or use the Indians to eliminate the Conquistadors of your opponents.

A battle for survival between the creatures of the Magic Wood. Units are captured by being surrounded by opposing pieces or neutral animal pieces. And remember, the "shortest player goes first."

description:
Chess for three players on a hexagonal board. Includes a standard chess board as well. Designer may be Robert Zubrin, as he is listed on the box and rules as the copyright holder.

Edit: I talked to Robert Zubrin at the 2011 Mars Society conference and he mentioned that he designed a chess variant for three people.  He should be listed at the designer.

Played on a three dimensional board game, Miners' Mountain, for two to seven players, takes you back to the mining days of the Klondike era. The object of the game is to become the wealthiest prospector.

A dice roll moves your game pieces up the mountain until you reach one of the numbered mine entrances. Placing a numbered marble in the mine entrance enables player to actively mine that mine. To recover gold, a player rotates the mountain using mountain movement cards. Amount of payment for gold depends on marble landing in assayer's tray. Once paid, the player reuses the game piece, to start over climbing the mountain to mine for more gold.

More advanced players can challenge each other to gunfights or steal each other's mines by claim jumping. A dice roll determines who wins a gunfight or takes over a mine.

A third dimension to the game, Bushwhackers, is a game of capture. It uses the gunfight rules along with the location on the mountain to gain advantage against your opponent.

Dream On is an exciting new board game that answers the age old question 'What do my dreams really mean?' The game provides over 1000 dream interpretations derived from the works of the world's most prominent dream analysts."

boardgame: Lost Worlds Adventure Set II: The Dwarf and Goblin Wars
description:
"Mortal combat as Dwarves and Goblins meet in heroic battles in the deep caverns of the world!"

The second boardgame based on the Lost Worlds series of fighting gamebooks. Players move miniatures around a 3-D plastic molded cavern, and maneuver on the board until they came into melee range, at which point they switched to the Lost Worlds books to resolve combat.

Rules were provided for movement, ranged combat and furniture. Components include Ral Partha metal miniatures, treasure cards, and ten scenarios. Also included are the Lost Worlds books for the Dwarf in chainmail with a two-handed axe, and the Giant Goblin with a mace and shield.

Part of the Lost Worlds series.

boardgame: Trade Wars: The Game of Global Commerce
description:
The Cold War is over! Now the Trade Wars begin.

Trade Wars the board game is a microcosm of the real Trade Wars raging outside, driving global economic conflict home.

The Trade Wars board simulates the globe, and is divided into four geographic regions that a player may control. Players move through the regions of the board purchasing corporations, forming Trusts and Cartels, and collecting license fees from opponents. Players risk making unsound investments, paying license fees to other players, and encounter trade and tariff barriers. Players seek to create transnational corporate empires and either buy-out other players or run them out of existence. Each region has its own currency and players must deal with rising and falling currency exchange rates. Market Force cards randomly effect the game. Players may buy and sell corporations to other players or the bank.

This game has a player pre-determined length of play with variable playing options. Also of note, this game comes complete with player aids and a Teacher's Guide for classroom play.

Instructions and 52 cards to be used with any Parchisi-style game. Now a player may draw a card after a move or play a card instead of rolling the die. There is a hand limit of 5. The illustrated cards give special powers and effects.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Interpretation of Dreams lets everyone play amateur psychiatrist in an unbelievably funny game where with the aid of a specially compiled Dream Dictionary you will get to know yourself and your friends better."

Each player writes down an account of a dream. The moderator collects all the sheets and reads a dream aloud to the group without identifying the author. The group then identifies the symbol words in the dream and these are looked up in the Dream Dictionary. Each player writes an analysis of the dream, which are read aloud to the group. The players also write down a guess as to who they believe wrote the dream. The winner is the player who has the most correct identifications.

Touch, Parker Brothers' game of palmistry.

"Maxine Lucille Fiel has studied scientific hand reading and palmistry for 15 years . . . Maxine claims that the hand is like a graph that reflects the emotional and physical changes in the body.

Touch is intended as a group game for parties or other gatherings. It can, however, be played solitaire since it is basically a detailed guide to palm reading and analysis."

The game includes a set of oversized cards illustrating different categories of markings on the hand and a pad of Profile Sheets for recording which markings appear on a player's hand. The personality characteristic for each type of marking is listed in the game booklet.

The brief rules suggest that players break into groups and work on figuring out the what markings appear on their hands. Once these have been marked down on profile sheets, all of the sheets are gathered and the interpretations are read from the game booklet. The rules suggest that you read the analysis aloud and let your friends try to guess who is being described.

The card bowling game is scored exactly as in the regular rules of bowling and playing technique is based on official bowling rules.

Typical bowling score sheets are included.

Ten "frames" are played. High score wins.

Players are dealt a hand of five Bowlo cards.

The deck consists of 54 cards:
18 Ball cards.
12 number 1 Pin cards.
12 number 2 Pin cards.
12 number 3 Pin cards.

Any number of these five cards may be discarded and new cards called for to retain a total of five cards, which every player must hold in order to score.

Should the original five cards held total 10 points a "strike" is called.

After a second hand or a draw, should a 10 be scored, a "spare" is credited.

An "error" is scored for any player who scores less than 10 "Pins" in two hands (or draws).

A "bust" (zero points) is scored when a player has a hand totaling more than 10 "Pins."

A game of yacht racing, and an interesting precursor to 3M's Regatta. Sail Away was possibly designed by Howard J. Mullin as he is listed as the copyright holder on the game board.

Sail Away is played on a board resembling a nautical chart overlaid by a square grid. The game includes six metal yachts numbered 1 through 6. The yachts lean to one side or the other according to their tack. The yachts are moved across the map using the intersections of the grid lines.

Movement is governed by a spinner which has an inner and outer circle of results - one circle for the upwind leg, and the other circle for the downwind and crosswind legs. In addition to the bit of luck injected by the spinner (reflecting the vagaries of the wind), there is also a slight chance for misfortune - two small spaces on the spinner allow for a player to lose a turn for having a man overboard and two turns for capsizing. The rules also cover blanketing effects by windward yachts toward leeward yachts, tacking, and jibing.

The course is somewhat fixed in that the wind direction is always the same (southeast) and there are three course markers designated on the map. However, there are four different courses that may be run: counterclockwise around the triangular course described by the three markers, clockwise around the triangular course, around course marker 2 and back, or around course marker 3 and back. In addition, players may choose to race one lap or multiple laps.

"Consider yourself the Captain of an airliner on a flight from Miami, Florida to Seattle, Washington. The object of the game is to pilot your aircraft along the airways, crossing over the cities of your choice, in route to the destination airport and be the first to complete the flight."

"This game is designed to be a realistic experience for the player, as many of the game rules are based on actual federal air regulations."

"Pilot-players pit themselves against each other and other flying odds as they strive to stay in the lead. Each pilot must be careful of thunderstorms which can unexpectedly cross their paths, just as in a real flight. Each pilot might also hit strong headwinds, severe icing or even clear air turbulence, as they fly across the country."

Straightaway offers a strategic take on Grand Prix auto racing, placing players not in the driver’s seat—but in the sponsor’s suite. Each player acts as a high-stakes sponsor backing professional race drivers as they compete for victory across a multi-lap racecourse.

Instead of traditional driving mechanics, Straightaway uses a “computer-style” race tracking format, simulating the action turn by turn. Players invest in drivers and must track performance as cars navigate high-speed straightaways, sharp turns, and mechanical risks. Every gear shift and maneuver could mean profit or loss for the sponsors.

The game combines the excitement of Formula One with financial strategy, offering a unique blend of racing thrills and business decision-making. Suitable for fans of racing and economic-style games alike.

"Your mission, should you choose to accept it, is to seek out your opponents and devastate them financially. You will assume the disguise of an attorney and the alias of your choice (Scooter Cohooter, Kid Fleece, L. Gotcha, or Sam Boozlebam). The rules and suggestions are only guidelines; you may operate on the principle that anything you can get away with is legal. In order to preserve the spirit of Hornswoggle, I recommend no mercy be extended to those players in dire financial straits."

Hornswoggle was designed to capture the essence and true spirit of the legal system. Certain modifications were necessary to make the game function logically (unlike the real thing)."

"Objective: To have the most money on deposit in the Swiss Bank after the game ends. You will accomplish this by suing your opponents. One third of your profits will be deposited in your Swiss Bank account."

A simple sailboat racing game where the players race along a track around the board. Movement is governed by a spinner. Players may be confronted with trivia questions (regarding sailing) during the game - a correct answer moves a boat forward and an incorrect answer moves a boat backward. Additional cards are drawn during the game which provide further information regarding sailing.

Dubbed "A Tactical Sailing Game", Windshifts uses a novel game system combined with unusual components to recreate international yacht racing.

The board is randomly created as the race develops using 19 plastic map cards that lock together. The race is in three segments; a windward leg, a downwind leg, and then another windward leg to the finish line. As portions of the race are completed, the map cards from that segment are returned to the draw pile to be reused later in the game.

There are no spaces on the board. The direction and strength of the wind are indicated by lines on the plastic map cards. The distance a ship moves is determined by the wind strength and the ship's bearing in relation to the wind direction.

The ships have clear plastic bases with a numbered wheel mounted in the center. The ships' bases have lines molded into them that are aligned with the lines on the map cards to establish the bearing of a ship. As the ship is moved across the map, the wheel in the center turns, and each number on the wheel indicates one unit of movement.  On a map card with strong wind indicated a ship bearing on a beat, broad reach or downwind moves 4 units or one complete revolution of the wheel.  A beam reach bearing generates 6 units of movement.  On a map card with light wind indicated, the movements are half.

A plastic "windshadow" card is used to determine when an opposing boat is in "disturbed air" - this differs based on whether the boats are on the downwind or windward (upwind) leg.  The sail of a ship will cause the air downwind (behind) of it to move in weak chaotic swirl patterns, the sail effectively blocks the wind from supplying power to any other boat following downwind  within about 1 1/2 boat lengths.  In the game, the "windshadow" card is used to determine if one boat has blocked the wind from powering another boat.  If this is the case, the movement of the blocked boat is reduced.

boardgame: AirLand Battle: Corps Operational Command in Europe
description:
AirLand Battle: Corps Operational Command in Europe is an operational level wargame that pits an American corps against a Soviet combined arms army which is reinforced by a second echelon tank army.

Players control additional air, artillery and helicopter assets.

This hex and counter wargame features limited intelligence, doctrinal tactical formations, organic supplies, missile and artillery ammunition restrictions, electronic warfare capabilities, chemical warfare, night combat restrictions, special light infantry capabilities, air interdiction and close air support units, refit of units and more.

description:
Battle Cry! is a man-to-man wargame covering infantry battles on a tactical level between US Marine units and the Imperial Japanese army units. The scale is 10 metres from hexside to hexside and each counter represents a single infantry.

Four maps are provided which simulates the typical terrain found in the Pacific War. The maps include a beach map, jungle map, mixed terrain map and rough terrain map.

Each individual unit will have a performance rating (PR) which requires that a performance check (PC) be made before moving, firing, observing or assaulting.

The advanced game includes armoured fighting vehicles (AFVs), crew-served weapons, anti-tank weapons, mortars, flamethrowers, mines, hidden units and more.

description: This is a two - to four person tic-tac-toe-like game using a four level circular board.  You try to complete a line of four in your color or an "orbit" in your color. Interesting "twist" on a rather basic game - interesting ability to add additional players - helps mitigate first player advantage.

From the back of the box:

VCR Baseball is the exciting baseball strategy game that can turn an ordinary afternoon into a thrilling day at the ballpark.  Become an actual player and see the outcome of your hits on TV as you watch the video tape containing footage of professional slugging action.  Home runs, double plays, triples, fielding errors, pop-ups...it's all there for you to see and experience!

The basic game is quickly learned and fast playing.  Each time you pitch or bat you choose a baseball strategy.  Make your decision just as in real baseball -- depending on what kind of hit you want to get, how many outs there are, and if there are runners on the base.  Then compare your choice with your opponent's and find out what happens.  You could strike out, get a walk, or a hit.  If it's a hit -- you watch the videotape to see the outcome!

And VCR Baseball is actually two strategy games in one.  The advanced game adds more baseball depth.  Your team players have their own batting and pitching abilities, so you can bring in a right-handed pinch hitter to try for a crucial hit off a lefty pitcher.  Or relieve a tiring pitcher.  Intentionally walk an excellent hitter, bunt or steal a base.

Features:
- 2 games in one!
- unlimited replayability
- for 2 players
- live footage of professional ball players in action
- fun for ages 12 to adult

Components:
- approx. 30-minute video tape
- colorful game board
- 6 playing pieces
- batting and pitching cards
- playing grids
- score pad
- player cards
- 3 dice

From the back of the box:

FIBBER! is the family fun board game with a great new twist -- when players announce what number they've rolled, how can you find out if they're telling the truth when you can't see their die?  By challenging, that's how -- Just say "Are you a Fibber?"  Off comes the top of their dice shaker and if they were fibbing -- whoops -- back they go; but if they were telling the truth -- then back you go!

From the back of the box:

Everybody wants a TAMAGOTCHI toy, but not everyone has the devotion to raise one properly. Here's a perfect solution: The TAMAGOTCHI Game. Play this game with your friends, (with or without the TAMAGOTCHI toy.) Just keep your pet well-fed, happy, healthy, clean, well-trained and amused...before you know it you'll have a full-grown, fully nurtured pet!

Everything you need is here: 4 TAMAGOTCHI moving pieces, 72 nurturing cards, 4 growth meter and an exciting game board. The TAMAGOTCHI Game is a terrific way to enjoy some real interactive quality time with family, friends, and of course, your favorite pet!

boardgame: Escort: The Game of Guys and Gals
description:
Object is for the player to move all three of his 'Gals" from their starting places on the board and into their "homes" on the opposite side of the board.

They're escorted home by the nine 'Guys' of each respective player.

Opposing 'Guys' can be captured by jumping.

'Guys' & 'Gals' can move in any direction, backward, forward, left, right or on diagonal.

'Gals' cannot jump and can only be used to block moves.

First player to succeed in getting all three of his 'Gals' into their 'homes' on the opposite side of the board wins the game.

boardgame: Screwball: The Mad, Mad, Mad Game
description:
Every player or 'Boob' is in the "MADvertising business" on MADison Avenue. He drives his Jokeswagon along the path, collecting "clients" and slogans. Meanwhile he spends his Mad Money.

First Boob to lose all his Mad Money ends the game and he is the winner.... Unless... the richest of the remaining players holds in his hand the "Madly Rich" slogan - this player is then declared the winner.

Supposedly this game was at the center of a Mad Magazine lawsuit which Transogram lost. I haven't been able to find anything substantiating this.

boardgame: Campaigns of the Civil War: Vicksburg and Chancellorsville
description:
Campaigns of the Civil War: Vicksburg and Chancellorsville is a wargame that simulates two critical events of the American Civil War through a game system with a map divided into strategic squares.

While this game is designed especially for solitaire play, it can accommodate a second player.

Individual counters represents divisions, brigades, leaders, ironclads, transports and a handful of companies and battalions deployed in probing operations.

A Combat Results Table (CRT) and a six-sided die is used to resolve combat. An 8-page rulebook and 17"x22" double-sided map are also used.

description:
Object of the game is collect as many illness sets determined by the players.

In his/her turn, each player draws and Illness Card. He then places the card face up in front of him. The player then draws an Herb Card. He then has one chance to identify one illness that is listed on the Herb Dial under that Herb.

If the player is correct, he may retain the Herb Card.

It the Herb Card matches the illness of the card or cards he has, the player has the option of placing it under that illness card or saving it for other possible illness sets.

Winner is the player who first completes the predetermined number of Illness Sets.

Legal Decision is a challenging game for Ages 14 and up. A game like you have never played before -- all the excitement and suspense of a courtroom trial! You can be the quick-witted defense attorney, State's tough prosecutor, jury foreman, or the judge himself! Witnesses testify, cross-examination may change testimony - jurors' sympathy can be swayed, new evidence may be introduced. The drama builds as the jury moves toward its verdict.

The designer is likely Rico Gatti who is listed as the copyright holder.

The People's Court game is based on the very popular television show where the litigants settle their disputes in Judge Wapner's courtroom. An exciting game where the players are plaintiffs trying to win cases by landing on 'Case Cards', such as Problem Pets, Neighborhood Feuds, Disservices and Shoddy Goods. And, just as on the TV show, Judge Wapner is the ultimate authority."

Welcome to West's Great American Case Race Game. The objective is to have some old-fashioned fun as you learn the advantages that determine success in computer-assisted legal research. But that's not all. It's a fast-moving game, and it will take some strategy and thinking to win. You will have to make a lot of on-the-spot decisions. The challenge makes the game all the more fun! The rules are straightforward and most of the rules for the game are printed on the board itself.

—description from the publisher

West Publishing Company was the parent company for the Westlaw computer-assisted legal research service, which this game was apparently designed to promote over a competing service, Lexis. The game includes Westlaw and Lexis cards. The Westlaw cards usually score points for the player due to its purported efficient and easy-to-use services, while the Lexis cards typically cause the player to lose points because of purported 'difficulties' with the competitor's legal research service.

—user summary

You and your friends are the participants in the trial of David Defendant. The charge is MURDER TWO. After the attorneys and witnesses are finished with all the testimony all of you will become a jury to determine whether or not David was convicted or not. The testimony (facts) change from game to game so it's never the same.

Ready to practice law? ATTORNEY POWER (1982) will give you a corner office and as many clients as you can handle, but you have to give correct advice to real legal problems. ATTORNEY POWER will give you the opportunity to conduct a bar investigation, neglect to file a lawsuit, give advice on changing a will, decide where to sue, and many more real life legal problems. The game has paralegals, fee gougers and client stealers, as well as 56 evidence and liability rulings based upon recent court decisions in both criminal and civil law.

boardgame: The Story of the U.S. Air Force
description:
A  Landmark Game  based on volume 89 in the Landmark children's book series by Random House. For Ages 9 - 15

The game is a race starting from ground level to see who can be the first into orbit. The players start off with balloons and as they climb upwards various historical aircraft become available (including the Wright Flyer, Curtis Jenny, Spirit of St. Louis, P-51, F-104, and X-15). The higher the altitude reached, the more advanced the aircraft that may be acquired.

The game features colorful components with an interesting gimmick or two - for instance, the spinners are part of a box insert depicting an aircraft control panel, and the players use a miniature compass to try and get a correct reading on a radar screen box.

"Have you got what it takes to make it to the Executive Suite, or is Office Boy as high as you'll rise? You'll find out when you play THE BOSS, the fun-filled game that realistically recreates the gotta-get-ahead world of today's big business! You've got to be shrewd enough to make the wildest deals you can dream up -- or break a deal that's holding you back! You can buy 'n lend stock, sell 'n trade Experience, promise future favors (and fail to deliver!) -- yes, you can wheel 'n deal any outrageous way you can get away with! It's fast-movin', fast-talkin', fast-dealin' fun -- all the way to the top!"

PanzerForce is a realistic game that shows the problems faced by tank commanders in World War II. But it is much more than just a game. The two different tanks that will collide in battle will respond and perform as they actually did. You, the tank commander, will make the decisions that lead to victory or defeat. This is tank combat, the way it really was, a game of deadly adventure."

A game of tactical tank-to-tank combat where two Tigers face off against four Shermans. The game uses 6 HO scale plastic tanks.

boardgame: Magnetic Poetry The Game for Kids
description:
Be the first player to make it from the vegetable crisper to the freezer by win four poem-writing rounds.

One player is the judge, who spins a spinner to select the poem category (gross, sad, mad, pretty, silly, happy, gross) The remaining players write poems, based on a subject selected from their hand of subject cards, and using magnetic word tiles selected from the word board arrange them on their play board to form their poem befitting the category and subject. At the end of the three minute timer period, the judge selects the poem that is the best match of subject and category and declares the round winner, whose playing piece moves up one space towards the freezer on the score track. The judgeship passes to the left and play continues until one player wins their fourth poem round.

Sue You! was invented by a law student in the fall of 1991. He was intrigued -- and troubled -- by society's apparent fascination with lawsuits, and set out to create a game that would capture the essence of that fascination . . . The object of the game is to become the wealthiest player by successfully suing other players. The game ends when one of the players runs out of money. The player with the most money at that time is the winner."

"Play the Game. The book you can play. Complete with counters and detailed rules."

"Welcome to the exciting world of Board Games! From the earliest known board game in the English language, A Journey Through Europe, invented in 1759, through Edwardian England's Golden Age of Games, to the mid-20th Century, you will travel 300 years of history and pleasure. There are great new experiences for aficionados, as well as intimate games for two and party games for many."

This book includes color photos of the boards from 46 vintage games. Rules are included, along with a sheet of pieces to cut out, so that all of the games may be played.

List of games included

Great Board Games. Over 40 games that you can play. 1895-1955. Complete with rules and counters."

Similar to the author's earlier "Play the Game" book, "Great Board Games" is a collection of 43 vintage games that you can play. There is a color photo of the board for each game, rules, and a few sheets of game pieces to be cut out.

The first edition (Knapp and Cavendish) was published in 1979. It came in a slipcase and included four 20"x26" double-sided sheets with extra-large game boards. The second edition (Exeter and Cavendish) was published in 1983 and does not have the slipcase or the extra sheets. Both editions have a loose sheet with cut-out pieces for the games in the book.

"Play a boardgame known to the Pharoahs of Egypt, called Senat. Exercise your skill on Go, a boardgame considered an important part of Samurai warriors' education in the seventeenth century. Learn how to play Mu Torere, a popular tribal boardgame from New Zealand, or Konane, the game Captain Cook found the natives playing in Hawaii. Introduce your children to your own childhood favorites, like Ludo, Checkers and Tic-Tac-Toe."

"The Boardgame Book is a beautiful compendium of the world's most entertaining and universally popular games. Every board in this fascinating and unique collection of over eighty games can be played on the page, because the book has been specially designed to open flat. For easy reference, instructions with diagrams explaining how to play are on the pages adjoining each board. All the pieces and other equipment needed for play are supplied on a separate cut-out sheet."

"Here is a unique collection of over eighty games from every part of the world and dating as far back as five thousand years. Some, though very popular in their countries of origin, are almost totally unknown abroad, and others are rarities only to be found in museums and private collections.

Many of the games reproduced in The Boardgame Book are from an especially fine collection built up by R.C. Bell over many years. All the games have been chosen as much for their interest and visual appeal as for their value as a pleasant and universal pastime. About one third of the boards reproduced in this book are completely new designs which have been specially commissioned, with all the details taking account of traditional models."

Speed Dash!! The Great Sailplane Race.

Strategic decisions you make in The Great Sailplane Race are much like those in real soaring competition. Whether you stay on the main course or fly Cloud Streets, Ridge Lift, or the Wave depends on the situation, how your competitors are doing, and how gutsy you are. A word of warning: Speed Dash brings out the tiger in a pilot!

Part of the book and movie franchise of the same name, Rennschwein Rudi Rüssel is a simple racing game.  Each player has a hand of cards numbered 1 to 10.  Players simultaneously select and reveal a card.  Whoever plays the highest number moves their racing pig, but only as far as the amount they beat the second highest card by.  If two players play the same high card they swap positions on the race track!  There are also special spaces that allow you to exchange a card from your hand for a card from a face-up deck of additional cards.

Embark on an epic adventure of truly biblical proportions when you descend deep into the foreboding recesses of the labyrinth in search of holy artifacts.  Surmount obstacles and deadly pitfalls and survive encounters with fierce creatures and supernatural beings bent upon denying you access to the holy artifacts.  Witness history unfolding before your eyes as you find, and recover, holy relics thought to be lost to the ages.

Game is designed for multiple modes of play:

Single Player Mode
Multi-Player Mode (2-3 Individual Players)
Team Mode (up to 4 players per team)

Bonus Game: Quest for the Holy Treasure
Bonus Game: Mystery of the Number Seven

description:
From the publisher's website:

The Apocalypse is here!

The dead of the Earth have risen, and now they roam the land seeking living beings to feed upon. Powerful undead such as vampires and ghouls command legions of flesh-hungry zombies in chilling, inhuman war bands. Small pockets of resistance remain as humans wage a desperate battle for survival in a world where the waters have turned to blood and the sky is night everlasting.

Final Days is a complete boxed miniatures combat game where players command human freedom fighters or undead hordes in Earth's final battle! The combat system uses standard playing cards to determine the outcome of actions, and takes just minutes to learn. Play seamlessly with just two players or a group of eight or more! Everything you need to set up and play this fast-paced, hard-action game is included, and you can be up and  running in no time. This boxed set includes Twenty-Five 25mm metal figures (8 different brand-new sculpts) to recreate your intense tabletop battles.

So grab your shotgun and bar the doors. The dead are coming and nobody is there to rescue you.

Final Days is a skirmish-level miniatures game that uses a standard deck of playing cards, with jokers, as its randomizer.  Each figure has three attributes that determine how many cards it draws during combat, as well as determining how much damage the figure can take.  As a figure takes damage, his combat effectiveness decreases.  The game can be played with any number of players, and lasts on average 1 to 2 hours.

Box Contents

Rulebook
Scenario and Accessory Book
2 Double-sided B&W maps
Templates of burned-out cars, desks, dumpsters
2 Combat Trackers
Equipment counters
25 white metal miniatures:
Demon
Vampire
2 Ghouls
13 zombies
6 survivors
2 dogs

boardgame: Edelweiss: A Panzer Grenadier Scenario Book
description:
From the Avalanche Press website:
 German mountain troops fought on every front except North Africa, compiling an impressive war record (and a few war crimes as well). These elite “mountaineers of the plains” saw action in all sorts of terrain and in all weather.

Edelweiss is a 64-page book supplement for the Panzer Grenadier series, based on the battles fought by German regular army and Waffen SS mountain troops. It is not a complete game: playing pieces and maps are drawn from the following Panzer Grenadier games and supplements: Elsenborn Ridge, 1940 - The Fall of France, Arctic Front, Road to Berlin, Battle of the Bulge, Sinister Forces, Eastern Front, Afrika Korps and Desert Rats. There are 41 (forty-one!) scenarios, each based on an actual battle or situation, plus historical background on the German mountain divisions and their exploits.

Edelweiss includes 161 laser-cut and mounted playing pieces: 157 of them the standard 2/3-inch square pieces of the Panzer Grenadier series, and four of them double-sized monsters. Two are Soviet river monitors, and there are two versions of the giant motorized mortar "Karl."

boardgame: Arctic Front: Finland's War, 1939-1944 – A Panzer Grenadier Scenario Book
description:
Adds Finnish troops to Panzer Grenadier. 20 scenarios and 88 mounted and die-cut counters with Sissi ski commandos, Finnish-made BT-42 assault guns, Soviet SMK heavy tanks and Aerosans (An aerosan is a type of propeller-powered snowmobile, running on skis, used for communications, mail deliveries, medical aid, emergency recovery and border patrolling in northern Russia, as well as for recreation.) Aerosans were used by the Soviet Red Army during the Winter War and the Second World War.

Not playable by itself, but an expansion for Panzer Grenadier: The Eastern Front and Heroes of the Soviet Union.

description:
TerraTopia was released by The Nature Company chain of stores as a product for their children's group, Kids' Club for Nature.  There was a comic book about the characters used in the game, a group of five kids who each had the ability to turn into a different animal.

The game is cooperative.  The kids have been placed on the planet TerraTopia and have to each find their colored stone before the planet's three moons meet (or something along those lines).  The players can choose to travel around the board together or separately.

The game was designed by Peter Olotka, one of the Eon Products team, and his son Greg.  The game definitely shows some of the Eon originality in the mechanics.  The players have to recover their stones by overcoming challenges at various locations on the board.  There are a large number of possible challenges, generated by randomly placing generic challenge markers (e.g., "Fog") at the various locations and having a booklet which describes exactly what Fog means at each location.  The players have to be able to explain to each other how they would use their character's "powers" (shape-changing and normal talents) to overcome the challenges.

RISK in the "Star Wars" universe. The "War" in the title is how Risk is called in Brazil. In essence, it is the same game, with planets as territories. Apart from the map being as seen in a mirror, the only difference in its layout is an extra link between two planets. For some obscure reason, the game is limited to four players - maybe because they ran out of cool colors, as they used black, white, gold and silver.

description:
A 6-suited deck of cards (adding the blue suits - wheels and rackets) specifically designed for playing Bridge with six players.
In my set there was a copy of the rules to 6 suited Texas Poker, with a list of frequency you can expect. And there were rules for 6 suited Texas Gin Rummy.

Be the first player to get past the obstacles and make it to the finish line. Players start off as Bruce Banner and get the chance to turn into the Hulk and race across the board with extra speed. Features Banner-to-Hulk transformation piece with a photo of Eric Bana as Bruce Banner and Dale Keown Hulk artwork

Fought on January 23, 1879, the Battle of Rorke's Drift could well be called the battle of the Victoria Cross. Of the one hundred thirty personnel involved in the defense of the station and hospital at the Drift, eight of them received this most coveted of all British decorations . . ."

description:
This game of ship to ship combat takes place on a mapsheet depicting the surface of a large space station dotted with gun towers. There is a trench down the middle of the map with a target at the end. The good-guys are called the Rebels. Their ships are Red Leader, accompanied by Red Two through Red Eleven, and Gold Leader, accompanied by Gold Two through Gold Five. There's also a Pirate Freighter. The bad-guys are called the Empire.

If you haven't gotten the picture yet, the cover art is a drawing of an X-Wing shooting at a Tie Fighter, straight out of Star Wars. This independently produced (and unauthorized?) game depicts the climactic attack on the Death Star . . . I mean, on the "Imperial space station".

This game is part of the Star Wars Games.

Pacific Theatre Via Midway is a variant/expansion to the Midway wargame (1975 Avalon Hill) that appeared in The GENERAL Vol.15,No.5, Jan-Feb 1979.

The basic idea is each player has his or her own map of the area (in this case the South Pacific) behind a screen. Each player "searches" for the enemy fleet by flying search planes to the hex and calling the hex number. Once found, bombers can be sent in to sink the enemy fleet. The actual attack is fought on a separate battle board where the ships are first placed on hexes and the attacking planes are placed in hexes to indicate which ship it is attacking.

The basic game is very simple. But there are many additional rules, which add realism (and complexity): CAP fighters, surface actions, bases, inaccurate search reports, etc.

The game is superior with a neutral referee for true double blind play.

Note: the rules and maps for the Coral Sea Variant were published in General Magazine Vol. 15, #5, counters were available separately via ordering through Avalon Hill.

Players represent miners digging for gold under dangerous circumstances.  The gameboard itself is printed on the bag which houses the tiles used to create the earth and treasures within.  Beginning with a debt to the bank of ten gold, miners purchase equipemnt from the store -- ladders, backpacks, dynamite, etc.

Its time for a picnic.  With the food, the players also bring an assortment of fly swatters and bug spray to defend against the hordes of bugs sure to come.

The starting player pulls a bug card from his hand, and passes it to the next player; who can either play another bug -- passing the larger horde on to his neighbor -- or defend his food with one of the aforementioned weapons.  If he fails to do this, he loses food.

The game comes in two editions -- the standard which is comprised of 114 cards and 24 food tiles; and the deluxe, which features 138 wooden tiles.

The Improv Comedy Game is based on the TV show and actual comedy club, The Improv.  It tests your ability to make your friends laugh through various actions and jokes.  The game contains 4 booklets, one each of "Gags", "Improvs", Groans," and "Cracks", which are used for each player's turn.  Players move around a board that is encased in the game box, using plastic pawns.  One accumulates points by successfully getting the other players to laugh at the jokes one performs, and can lose points by landing on spaces such as "heckler funnier than you," "audience speaks no English," and "steal material".  There is a pegboard in the center of the game board to keep track of each player's score; players move their pegs point by point from "beginner" to "star", and the first person to become a star is the winner of the game. The theme of the game is running, and takes place in three stages. Each player tries to  manage their three racers through training, qualifying, and then during the finals.

Each runner has  three different qualities..

During training you roll dice and choose a pawn to advance, trying to land on beneficial squares and avoid landing on detrimental squares. The  goal of this stage is to get tactic cards to help you win the qualifying race and the big final race.

In the qualifying rounds you roll dice and play cards in an attempt to get one of your better players, with more tactics cards qualified for the final. You are always guaranteed a place in the finals even if you fail to qualify (you get in on a 'technicality'). If that happens though you get no cards and no special abilities.

The finals is a more detailed race - with a race track, blocking, sprinting, and so forth.

description:
The gameboard is an equilateral triangle consisting of thirty-six smaller equilateral triangles. Each triangle has a point value from zero to six. The closer to any corner of the large triangle; the higher the value of the smaller triangle.

The first player arranges counters in piles on any of the zero point triangles. Players alternate turns moving stacks of counters with the object being to move the counters off the corners of the large triangle.

Once the players have decided on a target score. This target number is based on a dice roll. The twenty-one letter cubes are rolled and, in turn, the players decide which letters cannot, can and must be used. The player who rolled must use the permissible cubes to make a word (or words, in crossword fashion) to reach the target score. There is a sand timer to limit the interval spent on thinking.

ON-SETS is an exciting and colorful resource allocation game that teaches set theory–the foundation of modern mathematics. Players in ON-SETS explore the concepts of union, intersection, difference of sets, complement of a set, set identity, set inclusion, and the null and universal sets. ON-SETS captures the same creative problem-solving and strategic intrigue as EQUATIONS and WFF ‘N PROOF. The basic game can be taught to players as young as six; as players develop skill, the game becomes more sophisticated and ultimately expands to levels that will challenge intelligent adults.

description:
Constellation is an exciting space adventure game.  The year is 3001 and you are a finalist in the great Constellation Space Race.  As a spaceship captain you are dealt three of the 88 constellation cards at random.  Now the race begins...choose a pewter spaceship and roll the die.  To travel through space you either have to answer questions or follow actions.  By quickly docking on your constellations and being the first to return to the starting point you will be crowned "Space Race Champion!".

Game contains 1 double sided game board, 168 Playing cards, 4 English pewter spaceships, 2 Wooden alien spaceships, 12 wooden pawns 1 wooden die and 1 rules leaflet.

The goal of the game is to place as many as possible elements to the game field, which is a quad-grid 5x5 (but may vary). Elements are 2, 3 and 4 sided in all variations - 11 type of elements.
The game starts with the maximal element (with four sides) already placed in the center of the game field. Every next element can be put on the field, but only where the side of the current element can touch the side of an existing element.
If the side of a just-placed element touches the game field border, then the set of the connected elements scrolls in the opposite direction (or directions) if the element touches two borders. When this scrolling occurs, extra elements are deleted from the element set (they fall).
The player randomly chooses the element which must be placed, and also the element which will be next. In the computer version, these elements are randomly generated by the machine.
Every successfully placed element is counted.
The game ends when the current element can't be matched anywhere in the field.

The game is designed by Jordan Tuzsuzov.
Official internet site : http://devet.tuzsuzov.com/

In Blacks & Whites players experience life in the ghetto. Live on Welfare. Try to buy a place in a white suburb. Your challenge: To keep the land-hungry majority type from winning the game cheaply and quickly. Players were divided into Whites and Blacks and had to make economic progress while competing with each other. Based on "Monopoly", the idea was to demonstrate how the odds were stacked against black people in society by having different rules for each race in the game. The difference between this and all the other roll-and-move go-around-the board-buying-property games is that before the game, each player assumes the role of either a Black or White person.

Whites start out as the majority with $1,000,000.00 to spend and are able to buy property anywhere. Blacks start out as the minority with only $10,000.00 to spend; are unable to buy certain properties; have to draw from a separate Chance deck; are banned from purchasing property in the 'Surburban Zone'; and collect less money for completing a lap around the gameboard. Blacks are limited to certain areas of the board until they had accumulated at least $100,000.00. Players are encouraged to change the rules when anyone lands on the People's Park space.

description:
Tall Stories involves rolling two dice and moving a counter (shared by all players) around a board according to the result. The board spaces all describe themes. One die is red, the other is white. A card is drawn and the red and white dice specify two words on that card. A one minute timer is turned and you have that long to tell a story covering the theme you landed on and incorporating the two words you rolled.

It is the job of other players to identify which two words you rolled. The words of course bear no particular relevance to the theme and hiding them on the spot involves the creation of the most colorfully chaotic tales with impromptu decoy words thrown in as fast as possible.

The system of reward has gone lost to me, as have the rules, and I'm unsure of how points are scored, except to say that there is a 1-10 scoring bar in the game. How it is used I don't know.

I have written Crown and Andrews but they have been unable to find any records.

Hitchiker 2012 is an easy to play adventure where a little imagination, free thinking spirit and love of satire will take you for a wild ride on an emotional rollercoaster.

The premise is simple.  Players (2-6) assume the role of carefree Hitchikers and set out on the road to make their fortune while trying to avoid trouble along the way.  Hitchikers don't have jobs so you'll have to find some more creative ways to make your money.

Each player begins with a HOMETOWN, $25,000 and a Bank envelope.  The object of the game is to be the first player to save $250,000 in your Bank or accumulate $50 million in traveling cash.  You can only deposit money in your bank by passing through or landing on your own HOMETOWN.

The mechanics of the game (play method) will be familiar to anyone who has played a traditional board game like Monopoly except unlike most other games, you're never really out of it until someone actually wins.

Hitchikers travel by rolling two dice and following the highway clockwise around the board.  Each city or location along the road counts as one space.  Upon arrival at your new location, you will either be presented with an opportunity to earn huge sums of money or face a potential disaster like a Bad Ride or worse.

Although random luck (dice) will play a role during the game, there is also a need for strategic planning.  With two ways to win the game delivers an opportunity for everyone to have a chance.  If you tend to be cautious by nature, you can win by being conservative and saving money in your Bank.  On the other hand, if you like to gamble you will love the pursuit of monster payouts as you stack up your traveling cash.

Fast paced, high stakes action.

Once upon a time in a land far away, an Evil Witch captured a magical palace and locked its Good Queen in the tower! Can you and your friends help her escape? If you do, you can become Princess of the Kingdom!

Use the enchanted wand to search each palace room for a magic lantern, mirror, and key. When the wand lights up, you'll hear different sounds from each of the 7 rooms - birds singing in the garden, music from the music room, and even moans from the dungeon! Then listen closely and the Good Queen will tell you what to do.

Beware the Evil Witch - she'll turn anyone into a frog! Use the wand to undo her spells and save your friends! To win: collect all 3 magic objects, free the Good Queen, banish the Evil Witch, and become the Princess of the Kingdom!

Features a 3D board and mechanical as well as electronic parts.

This is a tile laying game that has math at its base. The object of the game is to be the first one to get rid of your numbers by making examples. There are three kinds of tiles: Number tiles from 0 to 9, equal sign tiles, and math function tiles (+, -, X, and /).

At the start of the game the number tiles are randomly drawn by the players until they have all been drawn. The function tiles are left face down to be drawn one at a time when its your turn.

On your turn you try to make a math equation using your number tiles and the function tile that you drew this turn.

Example you have numbers 0, 1, 4, 6 in your hand and you draw a + tile. On your turn you would  pick up an = tile and then play  4 + 6 = 1 0. The next player would then build off of the original equation either horizontally or vertically.
