The players would then consult the appropriate column to find out what happened.

Samsonite - yes, the luggage company - published a series of sports games in the late 1960's.  Each game comes in a very large box (approximately 15" x 22" x 3") and features card play on a board resembling the sports field or court.

In Samsonite Football, players play cards to attempt to move the football down the field and score a touchdown or field goal.  Each card contains different results for a different play call; e.g., "Rush: 8 Yards (3rd Down - 12 Yards; RB: 10 Yards (Kick-Off - 25 Yards); Pass... " and so on.

The name translates as "Ambush". Cowboys versus indians in the North American old West. This is one of the first two published games by the pioneer Brazilian game designer, Mário Seabra.

(Playing time is a wild guess.)

A easy to learn little dice game that asks you to Think Twice before you re-roll your dice. The game shares some similarities with Yahtzee and Cosmic Wimpout. You get 3 rolls of 6 6-sided dice and one special category dice. If you use all of your dice, your score doubles. If your dice match your category (high, low, same, different, odd, even) then your score doubles again. Once this score is made, you may chose to roll the Multiplier Dice. The Multiplier Dice will come up with 1,2,or 3, and this number is multiplied by your current score, but be careful, the multiplier die also has a 0.

A game of courtroom strategy. Each player is charged with a crime, arrested and forced to stand trial. OBJECT of the game is to be the first player to win 6 or 12 jurors and thereby be found 'not guilty.' This is done by landing on certain spaces on the board, seizing certain Stop & Frisk cards, and knowing your rights.

Trial Lawyer is subtitled "The Jurisprudence Game". Designer and publisher James Vail originally released the game under the name "Jurisprudence" in 1974. According to an insert in the earlier game, the original release of Jurisprudence was limited to 5000 copies.

Play either as a casino board game or as an add on for board games such as Monopoly.  Adds another layer to such a game.
---
Casinopoly: The Casino Party Board Game
Viva Las Vegas! And Atlantic City, too! With Casinopoly, players can play a casino party game or create an add-on feature that's compatible with "the most popular real estate trading board games." The board just happens to fit snugly into the center of a Monopoly board, and, whether you're landing on Boardwalk or cruising the Strip, Casinopoly's easy dice versions of four casino classics provide plenty of action. Played on its own, the game moves faster than the B&O Railroad: Land on roulette and roll the special die showing either red or black; slots dice roll to reveal a combo of cherries, lemons, and bells; toss the two card-faced blackjack bones to hit 21; or attempt to shake 7 or 11 with the regular craps dice. Should high rollers want to move from the Belaggio to Baltic Avenue, the rule sheet explains how to incorporate the games into a round of Monopoly. Casinopoly lets players go from starvin' to Marvin (Gardens, that is) with a roll of the dice.

You might have to act it out.....or draw a picture of it....or mime it.
It is the Secret Clue on the Mr. Bean QUIZ card - a person, a thing, a famous expression, a funny situation. And you, as Mr. Bean, have to get your team to guess what it is.
It gets very funny! You might have to say it - but without using your voice! Or write it - upside down, with the paper on someone's back - while they're walking away!

If your team guesses the Clue in time, you keep your place on the board. If not, back you go.

Even the rules are fun to read !! Tsk ! Tsk !

boardgame: Adventures with Clifford, the Big Red Dog
description: This is a cooperative children's game.  Clifford is traveling from home to the farm, and the players are telling the story of his adventures en route.  Each turn a player turns over a card that shows a picture on it (say, a fisherman carrying a fish).  That player then adds something involving that picture to the story (e.g., "Then Clifford saw a fisherman who had just caught a fish!")
family: Books: Clifford the Big Red Dog

description:
Samsonite - yes, the luggage company - published a series of sports games in the late 1960's.  Each game comes in a very large box (approximately 15" x 22" x 3") and features card play on a board resembling the sports field or court.

In Samsonite Basketball, players play cards attempting to move the basketball down the court and make a shot.  Each card features a different result based on what the player is attempting to do: for instance, "Shot from key ... 2 points and defensive foul"; "Shot from inside: offensive rebound"; etc.

Free your five discs from the frame to win...

The game consists of a plastic frame and some discs - a sort of cross between Connect 4 and Downfall.  Slide sections of the frame left or right to make the discs drop down a level.

Brainwaves a two-player abstract, fairly simple in concept.

Basically, it's a grid, and you've got 3 pieces on your side, and the other player has three pieces on their side.  To win, you have to move two of your pieces onto the other side.  Your pieces are all the same shape, in red, yellow, and green.  You roll a die, and then get to move one of your pieces that number.

The gimmick is this: before moving your piece, you choose which one you want to move, and then turn a hidden dial to that color.  Your opponent then turns their hidden dial to the color they THINK you're going to choose (or the color they want to stop you from choosing).

After both players have set their dials, a button is pressed, and the colors light up, revealing the choices.  If he guessed right, you don't get to move.  Otherwise you do move, and then the turn alternates - your opponent rolls and you have to guess.

There are passages on the board that can be blocked strategically, and you can't pass another token - but if you land on it exactly, you send it back home

There's a lot of double- and triple- thinking ("They think I'm going to do that, but instead I'll do this.  But what if he realizes that I know they thinks I'm going to do this...") to it, and it has a built-in balancing mechanism - when a player has gotten one piece in, they then only have two to choose from, which increases the chance of their opponent stopping them.

Most games take around 30 minutes, but with a lot of good guessing, the game could, in theory, go on forever.  I've seen one game take almost an hour-and-a-half!

Following a violent storm, certain marine birds became lost and disorientated: the aim of the game is to find the lost birds and re-group them. The game aims to improve children’s awareness of marine wildlife preservation, while keeping children surprised and amused by the original and innovative idea of the game, including an element of co-operation between players. Information cards are included for each of the eight marine birds used in the game, these have been made with the help of the LPO (Ligue Protectrice des Oiseaux). Three imaginary birds are also included in the game, leaving room for imagination.

Ana and her clan must save their talisman, Lacerto. Following a bad spell cast by Corvus Corax, he was transfixed into the form of a bracelet. However, Corvus Corax and his clan will do everything to prevent them and to guard their own talisman Vipera.
Ana contre Corax is an episode of ‘Ana, voyage dans les îles’. Ana is a child, who was born on the ‘Belle île’. She lives in an imaginary world populated by animals, equipped with powers and magical abilities.

Relive the ancient Course times, with a tormented historical episode, where the king himself elected the Corsairs. Its up to you to retrieve the torch of Jean Bart, René Duguay-Trouin, Surcouf…

Aim of the game: to become the richest and most respected of all Corsairs.

The internal war between the Tupamaros and the forces of the Uruguayan State security from 1968 to 1972 fought in Montevideo. The game is an unusual treatment in that because all the action took place inside a single large city, the map is a non-representational 'map of attitudes' of the people of the city of Montevideo, with an abstract troop and time scale. The guerrilla player has many subtle strategies to try while the Government player struggles to train his security forces and keep the people on his side.

The One Small Step Games edition is revised from earlier editions and includes:

     Two 11 x 17" playing surfaces
     100 die-cut counters 
     One rulebook
     Folio cover and counter storage bags

Box blurb: "Fast paced and exciting. An electronic football game that tests your football knowledge."

A smaller format version of the earlier "computer" sports games by Electronic Data Controls Corporation (EDC). Instead of the circuitry and buttons being placed inside an oversized table-filling board, Scrimmage used a small plastic board and console. The players are in control of competing football teams. The simple circuitry inside the game board helps determine the results of each play: yards lost or gained, interceptions, fumbles, etc.

EDC released a series of electronic board games under their "Computer Games" line, primarily consisting of two-player sports games. The series of games released in the late sixties used large wooden game boards depicting the playing field and a game matrix of various results. Each end of the game board had a set of buttons (i.e. offense and defense) that, depending on the combination pressed, would cause a light to illuminate next to one of the rows on the game matrix. The players would then consult the appropriate column to find out what happened. Scrimmage used a much smaller plastic gameboard which wasn't much larger than the size of the matrix.

Stop-Gate is a two-player game of perfect information. It can be played on a board or as a paper and pencil game.  As a board game, the game uses a checkerboard and a set of dominoes.  As pencil and paper game, players can take turns coloring in squares on a grid (there is no capturing or movement, so there is no need to erase anything during the game).

Stop-Gate is taken from the book On Numbers and Games by John Conway (Academic Press, 1976) and is attributed to Goran Andersson on page 74. In the book Conway doesn't really give the game any name.

It appears in Berlekamp, Conway, and Guy's Winning Ways for Your Mathematical Plays, where it is called Domineering, or Crosscram. There it says that "[t]his game has been considered by Goran Andersson", so it is not clear if the designer credit given here is accurate.

Stop-Gate is played with the standard winning condition for combinatorial games, which is that the player who makes the last legal move wins. A legal move consists of placing a domino on two unoccupied spaces on the board (or coloring in two adjacent squares). One player can only place dominoes horizontally, and the other can only place vertically. Typically, the game decomposes into smaller open regions, which may be of benefit to one player or the other.

An impartial variant exists where neither player has a restriction on the orientation of their dominoes. This is also called Cram.

From the back of the box:

An exciting new way to play with words.  Letters, instead of dots, turn this popular classic into a dashing, two-dimensional game.

Using basic domino rules, the object of the game is for players to take turns placing their lettered tiles "domino fashion" to build words and create a domino crossword.

Play ends when one player uses up all his tiles and the bank is empty.  Points are deducted for tiles left in anyone's hand.  And the highest score wins.

Inside, you'll find 50 tiles, each with numbered score points and two letters arranged horizontally on one side, vertically on the other.

Ages 10 and up
Game of bringing oil by ice-breaking tanker from Alaska to Humble City through the Northwest Passage.  Whatever happened to the S.S. Manhattan, the world's largest ice-breaking tanker?

The "Speed Circuit Tournament Tracks" are designed for road racing game enthusiasts who wish to expand on Avalon Hill's racing game, Speed Circuit.
The gameboard consists of two tracks: the qualifying track (normal track), and the tournament track (double track).
This module for Speed Circuit is intended to give the players more flexibility in track selection, an optional method for determining starting position, and a larger more exciting track for running tournaments. 
The packaging notes that the designers were the first and second place finishers at the Origins '79 Speed Circuit tournament.

A chess variant for 2, 3, or 4 players. Orbital Chess is played on a large circular board which is assembled from four large puzzle-piece style segments. The board is double-sided, with one side being used for two and four player games, and the other for three player games. There are separate rules folders for three player and two/four player games, plus folders with standard chess rules. As there are four sets of chess pieces, the game also includes two standard chess boards for regular play.

Copyrights are listed as 1969, 1970, and 1973 so there may be earlier releases.

It's your job to lead a population through four challenging ages of Civilization --- Hunting, Agricultural, Industrial and Atomic. Your goal? To survive the conflict between technology & nature and reach an ideal Environmental Age.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Unusual chase game.  One player plays the part of the husband, and others are relatives and friends of his wife. An elusive spouse appears and disappears from the board as other players roll dice to move about and see if they can land on the space where the husband is hiding.

Winner is either the husband or everyone else.  Husband is also given a level depending on when (and if) caught:  Aimless Amateur, Deflated Don Juan, Fearless Fugitive, Rueful Romeo, Careless Casanova, or Playboy at Large.

Very 1960's, but still amusing today.

Based on the video game of the same name, this card game has you build the highest scoring pyramid.  Players have a hand of nine cards, draw a tenth, and then play one of their cards either on their movement pile, their pyramid, or an opponent's movement pile.

There are three types of cards, movement cards, pyramid cards, and a few special cards. The movement cards show one or more diagonal directions on the pyramid the player's Q*bert may travel on future turns.  Once a movement direction has been chosen on a previous turn, players may add cards to build a pyramid one card per turn. Players may also play movement cards on their opponent's movement pile to send that player's Q*bert in a less productive direction, or waste another turn to change direction again.  There are also special cards which stop your opponents movement, or put Coily, Q*bert's purple snake nemesis, around an opponent's pyramid.  There are also helpful cards that either move a Q*bert to the top of the pyramid, or to allow free movement on the pyramid.

The finished pyramid is made of staggered horizontal cards.

Players play movement cards to change their direction on the pyramid until one player finishes the completed layout, and scores 100 extra points.  All players total their points on their pyramid and the highest score wins.

Rumble is a fast-paced game of creative superhero combat. Players invent new superpowers (with any effects they like) before the game then auction them off, the bid price balancing out any overpowered powers. Leftover auction currency ("energy") is then used to fight a simultaneous-reveal battle where players assign energy points to attack, defence and power usage, and are knocked out when all of their energy is lost.

Played tabletop, superpowers are written on index cards, and energy and bids tracked on paper. Online, it can be played through any real time chat system, with a moderator.

Additional scenarios and rules for the Aliens game.  Scenarios include the battle aboard the Drop Ship, Ripley's Rescue of Newt, the Marines' assault on the Lairs, an expanded version of the movie scenario, and the desperate hunt for the Queen.  Features rules that cover backup weapons, ammunition restrictions, fires, tournament rules, the Facehuggers, and the deadly Alien Queen.

Contents


33" x 17" color map
16 Alien lair cards
24 page rulebook


Expands:

    Aliens
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
The object of the game is to travel in time, back into different periods in the past or forward into some periods of time in the future in order to collect souvenir items from each of the 12 periods.
Components: 1 gameboard, 6 playing tokens, 1 sand-timer, 36 souvenir cards, 24 bad-luck cards, 12 time cards, 12 flight cards and two dice.

At first glance, this early Knizia title may seem like an easier version of Connect Four played on a 4x7 grid.  However, in Complica, when a piece is placed, it does not remain there for the entire game. Another piece may be used to slide the entire column down. The winner is the first person to have 4 pieces in a line. The mechanic of the sliding rows means that each player can possibly have 4 in a row at the same time, causing the game to continue until this situation can be broken up.

A new secret formula has been discovered! Only those in possession of this formula can properly prepare the super-secret Truth Serum which, while tasting like (gerstensaft),
persuades people to talk freely and reveal their secrets more effectively than any interrogation.

It isn’t exactly shocking that all of the Secret Agencies are all interested in this formula. Discreet offers have already been made at each one by parties purporting to peddle the real deal, but whether any of them are actually genuine is still to be determined.

As a Top Agent in your country’s Secret Service, your Chief has given you the assignment of obtaining the real formula and bringing it home – a difficult task, since
various Agents from rival countries are trying to accomplish the same thing. The identities and cover names of these rival agents are, by the way, also of interest to the Chief. To succeed in your mission, you’ll have to pull out all the stops: Search hotels for secret information, obtain entry into Embassies and Research Laboratories, install bugs and use informants.

This game captures the political and economical going-ons of the first German republic (the so called "Weimarer Republik") from 1920-33. Players take the roles of one of the major political parties (from communists down to the Nazis), trying to win elections and have their politicians in the government. Laws are made in a roleplaying style,  which change the rules of the game and influence the economics and life in Germany, while there are still reparations to be paid, and inflation threatens the economic stability.
Some parties have military and paramilitary troops which can be used to break other parties` rallies or to suppress public uprisings.
If the German Republic survives a certain number of years, the player, whose political goals are fulfilled, wins.

Itinerary is a game themed around worldwide air travel.  The board is divided into six continents, with six special, low value spaces around the edge of the board which don't belong to any continent.  Each plane can go on to two continents, plus the low value spaces.  Players roll to move around the world, and must land by exact count, but the nature of the board often makes it possible to reach a desired space with multiple different rolls.  To win, players must be the first to collect the required number of points in destinations.

In a post-apocalypse future (à la Mad Max), with scarce resources and few individuals, the survivor clans resolve disputes through ritualised racing instead of open warfare. You are one of these mad drivers. Only the first place counts, and no holds are barred.

The print-yourself components include 10 two-sided race track segments, six player cars and control panels (with tokens for shift gear and engine temperature), a 54 card deck (of additional car equipment) and armour, tires, brakes and turbo tokens. Five dice are also needed.

On your turn, you can speed up, slow down or change gears. This is cleverly handled by the control panel, where speed is vertical and gears horizontal - but not all grid points are connected. You can only go so fast in first gear! The gear diagram is also colour-coded to indicate how bad the engine heat test is. In addition, you may spend brake or turbo tokens (to slow down or speed up further).

You manoeuvre your car from lane to lane - but this depends on speed and your driving skill test. The faster you go, the less you can switch lanes. Tire tokens are spent for extra lane changes. If you ram an obstacle or another player, armour tokens are spent. Other cars could end up being pushed. A favourite technique is the Rush N' Crush, where you speed up right behind your opponent so as to crush him forcefully.

Kleine Monster (Little Monsters) is game for kids (5 years old +) where everyone will have to hunt monsters. Memory and speed will help you win!

Everybody has 9 hidden monsters discs. A special die is rolled, and then everybody tries to find a monster of the same color as the die. The first one who succeeds must take the big wooden green monster pawn and is rewarded by 1 monster disc. At the beginning you'll need luck, but as the game goes on memory (where are those little monsters hidden?) will help you.

Rules are available in English, German, French, and Dutch in each box.

One of the so-called "Penny Games" from tjgames, The HexGames are variations on both Reversi and Checkers, using a hexagonal board with hexagonal spaces, instead of a square board with square spaces.  The game is basically a board printed on heavy-stock paper, and a set of rules; you're supposed to supply the pieces for the games.

From the envelope: "Take a checkers board, grab it by the two sides in the middle, and pull really hard until you stretch it into a hexagon.  Now you have the basic idea behind the HexBoard Games.  A cheap Hollywood remake of two classic 8x8 board games, Checkers and Reversi.  If you really did try stretching your checkers board and it ripped ... sorry about that."

Hexversi requires 91 penny-sized pieces that are differently colored on each side.  Hexchex requires 12 standard checkers for each player.

From the box: The entire family will enjoy this exciting, easy-to-learn game.  Using simple addition and subtraction, players get rid of their cards and score points by making the running total reach zero or 13.  Bonus points go to the player who uses one of the Lucky 13 cards to 'take the trick'.  With strategy, quick thinking, and a little luck, you can be the first player to score 500 points and win the game!

The deck is composed of positively- and negatively-numbered cards, and the point of the game is to go out first, like Rummy.  Lucky 13 is not quite a set collection game, and it's primarily geared for family gaming.

At last, peace breaks out!  But, obviously, your previous allies will use the breathing space to re-arm, ally themselves with the enemy and invade us!  As the President, it is your sacred duty to beat the other countries to the punch.  The time for decisive action is now!

Each player has his own little country map, divided into conceptual areas: political, military, communications, production and population.  Each player also has a production card, 60 people tokens, 6 Action cards, 3 ships, 2 carriers and 1 airplane.  The players share 30 missile markers (power 5, 15 or 30) and 15 Goal cards.

The Goal cards represent your secret victory conditions (see below).  People will eventually spread not only over your own areas but the other players' --giving you a say in each player's destiny as will be seen below.

Play is in rounds, with each round beginning with a Player Order and Political phase, and then a varying number of player turns.

Player Order phase: The order of play is determined by each player's Communications area.  Each player counts his people in that area, and each other player with people in that area either adds, subtracts or stays neutral (his choice).  Largest score goes first during the round and so on.

Political phase: Place up to four of your six Action cards (keeping at least one), face down, in front of the other players, in any ratio.  This phase is resolved in sequence by all players.  Next come the individual player turns.

Production phase: Each player's production card is progressively filled up with people markers; the level reached indicates what can be produced.  Producing anything, even something way below the current "fill line", empties the card (the people go back to the player's Production area).

Embarkation phase: People of your colour (up to 10) may be embarked on one of your ships.  Missiles (up to 30 total power) of a controlled colour may be embarked on a carrier or airplane of a controlled colour which happens to be off the missile's shores.

Action phase: The player must play one of his Action cards (the ones he kept earlier plus those played on him).  Political clout (people in your Political area) can be used to instead lay a card aside for a later round.

These player turns continue until all players pass.  The players then recover their Action cards from the discard pile.  Cards laid aside during the round are put back in the player's hand --so you may end up with a smaller or larger hand than usual.  A player without any people in his Population area, or with less than 5 total people markers on his country (diasporas don't count!) is eliminated.

Goal cards indicate either a player to eliminate (you win even if another player is responsible for your target's elimination) or two players to dominate politically or demographically.

The Action cards are:

Transfer - Transfer up to 5 people of your colour from one area to another, within your country.

Disembark - Up to 5 embarked people on one of your ships off another country's shores disembarks into any area.

Growth - For each 5 people of one colour in your Population area, add two more.  Enemies also grow if present.

Production - Move up to 5 people from your Production area into the production display.  They may be of another colour if their owner agrees.

Move - You can move one ship of your colour, or one carrier or airplane of a colour you control by one country.  Ships and carriers move clockwise or counter-clockwise, airplanes move anywhere.

Shoot - Fire a missile on board a carrier or airplane of a colour you control onto an area of the country where it currently is.  The missile kills as many people as its power.  The more powerful missiles can spill their effect over into other areas of the target country.  Casualties must be fairly spread between the colours present in the area (if it matters).

From the box:

Ah, a Walk in the Woods... to observe beautiful things such as Wild Flowers & Butterflies... to help each other gather Goodies such as Apples, Berries, Nuts & Herbs... to keep an eye out for nasty stuff such as Poison Ivy, Stinging Nettle, Biting Bugs, even Hot Sun... to share our Remedies to solve Problems we meet... and to plan together where to look next for Goodies & Remedies. Let's have a great day in the Woods and come Home with our Baskets full of Goodies, our Minds deeply quiet, and our Hearts nourished with natural things.

boardgame: There's a Growly in the Garden!
description:
From the box:

Players help each other plant as big a Flower Garden as they can and also help the Growlies find what they need most, so they don't pick and trash the Flowers instead. With some sharing of ideas and talking over of strategies, we will help change Crabby, Messy, Greedy, Stinky & Snobby into happier creatures. Sometimes we will need Scarecrows as well as Special Items such as Fruit Soap, Cornstalk Brooms, Gold Coins, Smiley Buttons and, best of all, some delicious Humble Pie. Guess which Growly needs what item! On each turn, a player gets two cards to play somewhere in the Garden. When and where to plant a Flower? Or put a Scarecrow? Or leave a Pie? Decisions, decisions. Each game unfolds differently, so keep on your mental tiptoes. Lots of play value for as many as 6 players.

"Africanus recreates the two major battles fought in Spain by Rome's first great field commander, Publius Cornelius Scipio, later given the cognomen Africanus for his victory at Zama. Scipio is the first cog in the wheel that evolved the Roman army away from its highly linear velites-hastati-principes system to a more sectional and individual approach that would eventually become the cohort system of the age of Marius. His overseas victories and conquests also opened Rome's eyes to the possibilities of empire."

Battles:

    Baecula - 208 BC
    Ilipa - 206 BC


Expansion module for the Great Battles of History (GBoH) series game SPQR.

boardgame: The Great Battles of Julius Caesar: The Civil Wars 48-45 B.C. – Dictator
description:
Dictator introduces two key battles from the fading years of the Roman Republic, when Rome was faced with three major outside threats -- the Numidians under King Jugurtha; the Germanic invasion of the Cimbri, Teutons, and Ambrones; and the threat from the east of King Mithridates of Pontus -- and wracked with civil wars and the conflict for power between the two leading men at the turn of the century -- Gaius Marius and L. Cornelius Sulla -- all leading to the brutal dictatorship of Sulla. The outside threats led quickly to the establishment of the army as a political power base; the day of the military dictator was at hand, and the Republic was entering its death throes.

(from the publisher)
family: Series: Great Battles of History (GMT Games)

boardgame: War Elephant: Battles of the Diadochi 217-190 B.C. – SPQR Battle Module
description:
"War Elephant provides players with counters and rules for recreating two of the major battles of the Seleucid Empire, a legacy of the late Diadochoi period in Mediterranean history.

The Diadochoi refined and reformed the Macedonian system of warfare perfected by Alexander the Great. There were, however, two major factors that made their tactical efforts somewhat less successful. None of them was anywhere near Alexander the Great in their ability either to understand or to utilize tactical systems. And, they loved elephants (to be honest, so did Alexander, but he never had the chance to use them). Thus, while this period is the heyday of the Macedonian art of war (if not exactly its apogee), we find the system becoming overly rigid and ill-served."

Two battles:

    Raphia - 217 BC
    Magnesia - 190 BC (also included in SPQR (Deluxe Edition))

description:
"Pyrrhic Victory recreates the first two battles that the Epirote mercenary (and would-be Alexander of the West) Pyrrhus, King of Epirus, fought against the slowly but inexorably expanding Republic of Rome. It was the first time the Roman legions faced the Macedonian tactical system, although the phalanx as a military formation was quite familiar to them. It was also the first time the Romans encountered elephants. Although Pyrrhus appears to have won both encounters (there is some disagreement about the end results at Ausculum), the Epirote casualty rate was so high that it led to the formation of the term 'Pyrrhic victory.'"

Jugurtha simulates the two key battles of the Jugurthine War (112-105 BC), in which Rome expanded to take virtually all of North Africa, destroying, in the process, the once-proud kingdom of Numidia ... 
Jugurtha also gives gamers something they rarely see in ancient world battles: guerrilla warfare. Here's a rare chance to test your on-field maneuver capabilities. In addition, the Numidian forces are rather different: almost entirely light in nature, they rely on speed and mobility more than anything else.

boardgame: Diadochoi: Great Battles of Alexander Module
description:
Diadochoi covers the four major battles that took place during the early Diadochoi wars at the end of the 4th Century BC as Alexander the Great's marshals jostled and fought for position and control of his vast empire. Each of the diadochoi, the so-called 'successors', sought to expand upon and improve the Macedonian system of warfare so brilliantly used by Alexander. Ultimately, all they succeeded in doing was to rigidly formalize its elements, adding only hordes of elephants, the ultimate expression of which was the massive battle of Ipsus, the largest known elephant battle in history."

To play Diadochoi, you need a copy of The Great Battles of Alexander / Great Battles of Alexander, Deluxe Edition.

boardgame: Phalanx: Great Battles of Alexander Module
description:
Sellasia, 221 BC
Mantinea, 207 BC

Phalanx covers two of the major battles in the waning days of the Successor Wars, in which the Greek city-states and leagues and the descendants of the original Diadochoi were still fighting for Hellenic supremacy, and in which the phalanx -- the massive, heavy infantry formation brought to peak efficiency by Alexander -- was still in its heyday. The armies in these battles are very Macedonian in style, with a lot of mixing of unit types and capabilities, with the exception that, because of the terrain, reliance on cavalry is almost non-existent. The phalanx itself was still the army's anchor, although it was only a generation away from its initial comeuppance at the hands of a Roman army at Cynoscephalae.

To play Phalanx, you need a copy of The Great Battles of Alexander / Great Battles of Alexander, Deluxe Edition.

Mission: Grenada is a tactical level simulation of the U.S. rescue mission on the small island nation of Grenada. A unique game system of action points allows for great flexibility. Moderate complexity with playing times ranging from 1-4 hours depending on the scenario played.

description:
Christians and Lions is a game for two or three players, and in fact requires the participation of the Divine Being Himself. (Are you on His good side?) It somewhat facetiously recreates the dire plight of those first Christians, facing certain death in the arena. We suggest that for maximum enjoyment and playability the participants forget for the time being that feeding otherwise innocent Christians to a pack of ravening wild beasts is a vile and reprehensible act. At least that's the rumor going around. In any case, it's all in fun.

From the Fantasy Gamer review by Scott Haring:

"Let's face it.  If you're seriously concerned that Nuclear War trivializes one of the most important issues of our time, and if you sometimes wonder if fantasy role-playing games are warping the youth of America, then Christians and Lions may not be the game for you. Despite the warning on the cover (it says, 'The Irreverent Strategy Game,' right there in big print), you may not be prepared for what's inside.  Therein lies C&L's first hurdle - can anyone take seriously a game in which dozens of perfectly blameless innocents are ripped to shreds by marauding beasts? ...All this is very important in figuring the best strategy. What? Strategy?  Yes, Virginia, this game is not only borderline tacky and a barrel of laughs, its also a fairly good strategy game."  (Fantasy Gamer #4, Feb/Mar 1984)

The cards are full of historical trivia about the buildings and thus the game is certainly interesting in historical sense.

"An adult conversation game for 3 or more players"

Example of play:

Pick a Personality Card: You're Julius Caesar. (Don't tell anyone).

Announce your category: You're a Political, Military or World Leader.

Then get players to guess who you are by giving clever answers to Question Cards.

Q: If you were a type of clothing, which would you be?

You might answer: A toga.

Q: If you were a type of food, which would you be?

You might answer: A salad.

Contents:

From the instructions:

PerPlexus is a game requiring both quick thinking and a constantly changing strategy.  The play board is a network comprised of 6 sliding play pieces or shuttles that can be moved right or left, offensively or defensively, by either player.  The game objective is to place 5 stones in a row -- horizontally, vertically, or diagonally -- while blocking the opponent's attempts to line up 6 stones in a row.  Each shuttle has spaces for 7 stones.  PerPlexus requires that a player plan moves several turns in advance whether playing stones or moving shuttles.

Radio 1's Pop Music Quiz Game
Roll the dice to determine which question to answer. Answer the question correctly to score a point next to that number and another turn. Some questions have bonus points available. Answer six questions in a row for a 2 point bonus.
The game ends when a player scores at least one point against all numbers from 1 to 6. That player gets a two point bonus. The player with the most points wins.

Each player has to simultaneously make up words from the same set of letter cards which are dealt randomly. On each turn, all players are given one minute to select their best word, the longest of which wins the round and is written onto the playing board in a vertical or horizontal fashion. All subsequent words placed must connect, in crossword fashion, to those words already on the board. Points are scored for each letter written on the board to make up a word (as indicated on the letter card) plus bonus points whenever a bonus square is covered.

Icebreaker is an interaction board game designed for four or more players. The aim of the game is to score 2000 points. To gain points, players must perform forfeits. If players refuse to perform or co-operate with a forfeit, points are deducted from their score.

Join the world of Wallace and Gromit and their friends in a fun-filled game that'll have you doing rather odd things.
If you're Wallace, you have to pull your mouth into a grin every time you sing, say or name something. Gromit, on the other hand, can only draw.
Do the task before the time runs out... and you collect points. The player with the most points wins. Special gameboard spaces keep the surprises coming. You may get to switch characters, collect extra points or lose a turn as you take a "tea break". Each game is different and varied and the tables can turn at any time in this game of ups and downs and batty stunts.

Expansion for Space Crusade.  Adds ultra-heavy weapons and more powerful Chaos Dreadnoughts.

Inspired by the factory siege during the Stalingrad battle of 1943.

Expands:

    Space Crusade

From the box...
It's a reckless roller-coaster ride through the high-pressure world of big business booms and busts. That's Zzzinity, the game of chance and skill where anyone and everyone can play the financial market like a real entrepreneur. In this tense and exciting game for all ages, Zzzinity stands for the pinnacle of business achievement - and it's within your reach. With a little imagination, lots of intuition, zeal and creativity, you too can climb to the summit of success.

Players choose 6 pegs of a particular color and load them randomly - color up.

First player winds the timer, then pushes it and begins the game.

As they pop up, players turn as many of their own colored pegs over as they can before time runs out. The first to turn over all pegs of their own color wins.

Bases Loaded Baseball is a game from the APBA genre of table top, dice rolling, statistically accurate sports  games. The benefit of playing this game was that it was less costly than it's bigger named brothers and sisters in the genre. Problems with this game include rules that were lengthly and sometimes convoluted, game actions that seem to require two players (base stealing) and in some cases many, many die rolls to resolve a particular play. I have the 1961 season. It features every player that had 30 at bats or 10 innings pitched, so as far as having completeness of each team, the game excelled.

But having to roll the dice to check if the batter was HBP, or walked then roll the dice again to see is there was a hit, then again to see if it was a single , double, triple or home run, then roll the dice to see what field the ball went to, which fielder caught it, whether or not the fielder committed an error, roll the die to see if the pitcher holds the runner, roll the die to see if the 2nd baseman was able to turn the double play, roll the dice.......well you get the idea. The designer states "....There are some plays which require a lot of die rolls, but as you play the game you will realize an increase of play time and these extra rolls make the game realistic."
A difficult to find version of Pressman's many "Omino" games.  This one features 3-part colored dragons.  The object is to be the first player to use up all his Drag-Ominos.

Expansion for Space Crusade which adds the Eldar, their leaders, and weaponry. It is an expansion for two players which provides the "Marines" player with a larger squad (10 Eldar as opposed to 5 Marines (7 when using the Mission: Dreadnought expansion)) and powerful extra abilities for the Eldar commander to make a more balanced game.

Expands:

    Space Crusade

description:
Two Shamans battle each other to win the title "The greatest Shaman".

Each player has a set of numbered tokens (1-9) and some shell tokens. The numbered tokens are placed onto the 3×3 playing area, one of each player in each of the playing areas.

After all tokens are placed the battle starts, by choosing the place the battle occurs. One player secretly chooses the row while the other chooses the line of the battle. Then the two tokens in the battle square are compared - loser pays the difference to the winner in shell tokens.

Then one player may switch the position of two tokens. After this the next battle starts.

The winner is the Shaman who manages to collect all shell tokens.

Quite nice little W. Kramer game which was published together with Knizia's Nuba.

Also a variant and predecessor was presented in Reinhold Wittig's SPIELECOLLECTION No. 1 Spiele zur Schatzinsel(1988)
The first edition, in 1978, was named Check-Point.

"The G.E.V. and Shockwave supplement you've been waiting for: battle-ready replacements for scuffed-up counters and crumpled maps, along with four multi-map scenarios! New rules include towing options, random Ogre damage, and the variant Ogres previously only available in The Ogre Book. Fight to recover crippled Ogres on The Day After, deliver a crippling punch to a Combine industrial complex in Body Blow, or Fake Out the Paneuropeans by concealing your true objective until it's too late!"

A supplement for Ogre and G.E.V. which includes a full set of counters from each game and a set of the Shockwave supplement counters. Also includes the maps from G.E.V. and Shockwave which are used in the multi-map scenarios.  Expansion of the Game: Ogre / G.E.V..

Card game in which the object is to have more 'huevos' points at the end than the other players.   Try to steal your opponent's huevos or use the 'Hey! Get your hands off my huevos!' card to prevent theft.  A wagering element is introduced with the 'Huevos Challenge' card.

In Demontowers the players are warlords in a fantasy land whose identities are secret. They try to conquer some of the so called demontowers to win the game, and at the same time try to guess which lords the other players are to gain certain advantages over them. You can also reveal your lord to start a revolution in which a certain race defects to your side, so that winning is easier, but you have all the other players against you at this time.

The original Game version is only suited for 2-4 players. A 5- or 6-player-game is possible with an expansion, which comes with additional character-cards and all the other necessary stuff except for the missing dice-sets for the additional players.

Cabal is a two player asymmetric game.  One player is the Escaper and the other is the Trapper.  There is a main board which consists of a 9x9 grid of holes, and then each player has a board shaped to fit in the hand that represents the 8 spaces around the position of the Escaper's pawn on the main board.  The Escaper simply plots where they want to move.  The trapper has 3 colored pegs to put into his personal board.  If the Escaper moves to the position of the green peg, the move occurs but the green peg is placed on the board (anywhere except next to the Escaper); to the yellow peg, there is no move and the yellow peg is put down where it was pegged; and to the red peg. the move is blocked and the red and yellow peg are put on the board where they were pegged.

The game has 3 molded plastic boards and about 60 pegs in 4 colors. 3 sets of rules with slightly different forms of play are included.

Timelag is a strategic science fiction game of combat across the reaches of outer space where Einsteinian time dilation directly effects the flow of play in the game. As the years wear on, the warring civilizations reach higher technological levels. Subsequently, they produce ships which are better than the ships sent out previously. Of course, we are speaking of time relative to each player's home base. The ships themselves will have hardly aged at all relative to their Home Base. The concept presents interesting situations since one never knows for certain the 'age' of his opponent's ships.
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Translated from the box: "A young shepherd dreamed of a treasure hidden in the Pyramids of Egypt. He decided to follow the dream and, in the quest for his Personal Legend, faced great mysteries. The lessons of that journey are in this exciting game."

Based on the book by Paulo Coelho.

The object of the game is to be the first player to spell plug a jug.  Players take turns rolling a six-sided die.  The sides of the die show either a blank, a jug, or a cork.  Players take turns rolling the die.  When one player rolls a jug symbol they open the jug and shake out the small plastic letters and try to spell "plug a jug."  While they are doing this the other players takes turns rolling the die, until one of them rolls the cork symbol.  Once a cork symbol is rolled, the jug is plugged, and any additional letters are returned to the jug and the play continues until one player spells "plug a jug."  If, while shaking letters out of the jug, the black marble falls out, that player must return all of their letters from this and previous turns to the jug.  The jug is then plugged and play continues.  The game contains enough plastic letters (32) for four players to be able to spell plug a jug, the six-sided die, and the dreaded black marble.

The board for the game consists of a plastic tray with nine compartments arranged in a 3x3 grid for holding the playing cards.

The cards have either a blue circle or a green circle on them. To start the game, the cards are dealt into the tray, alternating blue and green on each row (i.e. Row 1: B,G,B; Row 2: G,B,G; Row 3: B,G,B).

The first player has to attempt to change the color pattern of the cards to 9 Blue Cards in exactly 9 moves. S/He may pick any card from any compartment and place it into any other compartment. this counts as one move. S/He may not take two cards from the same compartment consecutively OR place two cards into the same compartment consecutively. After s/he completes nine movers his/her turn is over.

For each card s/he has changed to blue, s/he scores one point. If all nine cards are the correct color, an additional nine points are scored.

The next player then attempts to do the same thing with the color opposite of the one used by the preceding player.

The first player to 100 points is the winner.

This appears to be a simple dice game produced to be handed out on an airline (American Airlines).

The centerpiece of the game is a tote board that cross references dice rolls with club selections. Players may choose from one of six golf courses identified in the accompanying brochure: Concord, Greenbrier, Torrey Pines, Canyon Country Club, Indian Wells, and La Costa. Each course is listed along with the yards to each hole and the par.

Each roll of the dice counts as a stroke and all shots are recorded on the included score sheet. Rolling doubles allows a player to roll again, possibly holing the shot.

The box bottom has a felt liner to allow players to quietly roll dice on those long airplane trips to the links!

From the box:

"Cover-Up is a game for the entire family. The idea is for each player to cover up as many squares and gain as many points as possible using the tiles provided for each player. However each tile played must touch another tile and cannot cover a black square. Each player must be careful not to give the easy plays to other players or not to leave themselves an unplayable position."

The game features a square board with 256 squares (16x16) on it. Some of the squares are blacked in, others have a number, while the majority are white and blank.

Each player receives a set of translucent tiles in one color (yellow, blue, green, or red). The tiles vary in size from one space to 2x2 spaces in size.

Players take turns placing tiles on the board in an attempt to cover numbers which increase scoring. Black spaces may not be covered.

A chess-like science fiction themed game. The game board represents the planet Elkos and is broken up into a series of hexes with four different types of terrain: Flatlands, Rough, High plateaus, and bunkers.

There are four different types of pieces corresponding to the four Elkosian "races" of the planet. The Etons move in a straight line and are blocked by anything but flat terrain. The Artilla Squads also move in a straight line, but may traverse flatlands and rocky terrain. The winged Ariel Bombers may traverse any terrain, but fly short distances - they also carry bombs to bombard the opponents. Finally, the Dominator Lord (one per side) moves up to three hexes but may not ascend plateaus. Its special ability however is to convert any opposing piece to its side by moving next to it.

An interesting twist to the game is that the game board may be altered through the use of bombs. When a bomb is dropped, the owning player may use one of 14 "tri-hexes" to alter the landscape, thereby making previously impassable terrain passable.

The game ends when either the opposing Dominator Lord is killed, one side no longer has pieces other than the Dominator Lord to move, or a stalemate is reached.

Game components are mostly card stock, three color printing on the board.

The object of each round is to connect and extend the "track" using as many track cards as possible before the opponent gets off the end of the track and escapes.

Hoo-Dee-Nee comes with 20 heavy-duty "track" cards each with a straight line, a 90-degree line or a "T" intersection. Each line has one or more red circles on it. On each turn, the track-laying player chooses one of three available cards to add to the board, extending or blocking a track. The opponent then rolls the die and moves that many circles on the track. The escaping player may double back on subsequent turns.

Scoring is simply the number of cards the first player was able to place before the opponent escaped from the board. Players alternate positions for subsequent rounds. A game is usually five rounds.
publishers: Research Games, Inc. / Athol / RGI / ARC

boardgame: How to Host a Murder: All My Children
description:
From back of box:
Eight of Pine Valley's most colorful characters await the arrival of their host at a dinner party held at the elegant Chateau Restaurant.  But high spirits soon turn to astonishment as Detective Trevor Dillon announces that their host has been murdered!  Which "All My Children" suspect is guilty of committing the crime?  You and your dinner guests will enjoy hours of suspense and intrigue recreating eight of daytime television's most tantalizing roles and figuring who among you is the murderer!  All you need is a sense of humor and a willingness to take a lighthearted attitude toward blackmail, sex, larceny, deceit and murder.  Just add eight suspects and the food.  This "All My Children" special edition episode of How to Host a Murder supplies the rest!

Based on the movie of the same name, with a blue cover depicting Snoopy and Woodstock. Standard track game in which players attempt to find Snoopy, and bring him home.

Players need to first bust Snoopy out of Lila's apartment where he has gone after visiting Lila (his original owner) in the hospital.  Then it's a free-for-all as everyone tries to take control of Snoopy and return him to the dog house.  Players can use telephone booths as shortcuts, use cards to cause the player with Snoopy to lose a turn or send a player to other locations on the board, or place a 'No Dogs Allowed' sign to block Snoopy's path.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Published in Abstract Games #14, and at the About.com boardgames website.

Components


     A triangular grid board with 5 vertices on each edge, with the three vertices in each corner marked off as "castles".  The base of the board has the West corner at the left and the East corner at the right. The peak of the triangle is called North.  The castles are Xanten in the West (the Netherlands), Worms in the East (Burgundy), and Isen in the North (Iceland).
     A set of chess pieces.
     Two screens.


Set-up Table




    
        Character

         Board Piece  

         Player Pieces 

        Sequence

         Start

    
    
        Gunther   

         white king   

         white rook    

           1    

         N Worms

    
    
        Kriemhild 

         white queen  

         white bishop  

           2    

         E Worms

    
    
        Hagen     

         white knight 

         white pawn    

           3    

         W Worms

    
    
        Siegfried 

         black king   

         black rook    

           4    

         N Xanten

    
    
        Brunhild  

         black queen  

         black bishop  

           5    

         W Isen

    


Concept

The forces of Tragic Destiny (Tod) and Natural Order (NO) are in opposition.

NO wins if Siegfried marries Brunhild or Kriemhild, which occurs if Siegfried is alone with either queen in a castle (both pieces occupy vertices of the same castle and the third vertex is vacant).

Tod wins if Gunther marries Brunhild, which occurs if Gunther and Siegfried are both in a castle with Brunhild; or if Gunther and Hagen kill Siegfried by the three of them occupying the neighbouring vertices on a triangle, with no vertex being part of a castle.

The victory conditions can be filled at any point in a turn, and the game ends immediately.

Set-up

Put each board piece on the grid point position listed in the Set-up Table under "Start".  Each player takes one of each player piece, and puts them behind the screen in front of him.  One player is Tod, the other NO.  Tod chooses 2 of his 5 player pieces and puts them on the table in front of his screen.

Play

Each player secretly chooses 2 of his player pieces, excluding those placed in front of the screen (i.e. on turn 1 Tod has a choice of 3 pieces, NO a choice of 5).  The player with 5 pieces may not choose the exact pair in front of the screen of the other player (either one may be chosen, but not both pieces).  The chosen pieces are simultaneously revealed, and put in front of the player screens.

Resolving Movement


     No pieces match.  Each board piece that corresponds to a selected player piece may move one space.  (Four board pieces move)
     One piece is matched. Each board piece that corresponds to a non-matched, selected player piece may move up to two spaces.  (Two board pieces move).
     Both pieces match.  No board piece is moved.


Board pieces are moved in the sequence given in the Set-up Table, by the player who selected the corresponding player piece.  Each board piece moves from point to point on the grid.  No piece may pass through another piece or share a grid point.

The player with 4 player pieces in front of his screen now puts all his pieces behind his screen.

The turn sequence is now repeated with players choosing two of their remaining player pieces, simultaneously revealing them and doing any allowed moves they choose.
Play continues in this way until one or other player wins the game.

The Story of the Nibelungenlied

The Nibelungenlied is an epic poem written in Germany about 1200AD, though its sources are far older.  It tells how the peerless prince Siegfried from Xanten in the Netherlands, comes to Worms to court the beautiful Kriemhild.  However, King Gunther and his two brothers jealously guard their sister Kriemhild.  Gunther wishes to win the hand of Brunhild of Iceland, but she will only marry the man who can win three contests against her mighty strength.  Gunther and Siegfried strike a pact, whereby Siegfried will help Gunther win Brunhild's hand and in return he may marry Kriemhild.  Using his magic cloak, Siegfried secretly assists Gunther to defeat Brunhild, both on the field and in the bedchamber.  Unfortunately, Siegfried keeps Brunhild's ring and girdle, and ultimately bestows them upon his wife Kriemhild.  A small grievance leads the queens to fight, the ring and girdle are produced, and Brunhild is humiliated.   She enlists Hagen, the chief vassal of Gunther, to help her get vengeance, and Gunther and Hagen murder Siegfried while out hunting.  Kriemhild swears to exact revenge, marries the recently widowed King Etzel (Attila the Hun) and finally invites Hagen and her brothers to Attila's court, where in a crescendo of battle and blood, everyone dies and honour is redeemed.

Siegfried and Brunhild are clearly destined for each other, and in older versions of the story he is betrothed to her, but is bewitched away by Kriemhild.  The Natural Order would be that Brunhild is won fairly and that Siegfried may marry any woman that he chooses.  The forces of Tragic Destiny twist the natural order so that Brunhild is tricked by Gunther and Siegfried, or that Hagen and Gunther murder Siegfried to obtain his magic cloak.

It's essentially Chinese Checkers with a rotating board.  The goal of the game is be the first player to move your 4 spaceships home.  During your turn, you can move 1 piece, by either moving 1 space, or 'jumping' over any ship or ships, checkers-style.

After you move, you then roll a die and the board spins from 1 to 3 spaces left or right! The end-game usually consists of everyone moving their last ship back and forth until a lucky die roll is made.

Note: There are separate game pages for the 1975 card game from Whitman and the 1974 board game from Omnia, as well as the Italian game Spazio: 1999.

Vintage HOME GOLF Pebble Beach 1961 GAME Includes Driver, Fairway Wood, No. 3 Iron, No. 5 Iron, No. 6 Iron, No. 7 Iron, No. 9 Iron, Wedge and Chipper PLUS Instruction Booklet.  Greens (made of Styrofoam). It's played in the comfort of your living room. The course is an accurate and detailed replica of the world famous Pebble Beach golf course in Monterey, CALF. Object of the game is to complete the course in the fewest possible strokes. The game consists of a set of 18 Fairways, a Putting Green, a Putter.    When a player's marker finally lands on a green, he actually putts across the felt-lined green (which takes up most of the box) into the little hole marked by the 8" flag with his little plastic putter.

This game is somewhat reminiscent of $25,000 Pyramid. A circular card with a visible category and 10 hidden items is placed into a viewer. A chart that is placed into the lower part of the viewer indicates which 2 players will play this round, and which will be giving the clues as opposed to guessing. The clue-giver presses a button to reveal each of the 10 items in turn and gives clues to the other player until the word is either guessed, or the clue-giver decides to pass. Passed words may not be revisited later. Once all 10 words have been gone through, or time runs out, each of those two players receive a point for each word that was guessed correctly. The next pair of players are then designated, a new category card is placed into the viewer, and the process is repeated until all of the pairings on the player chart have been completed. This tends to be a balancing factor, since all players get a round where they are paired with the better players, as well as the not-so-good players.

Each player must obtain a ship, captain and crew and attempt to take the loot from the treasure ship (or each other), bury it on Treasure Island and return with the map to Pirate's Cove.

Contents:

Gameboard
2 Six-sided Dice
4 Metal Ship Tokens
1 Gold Treasure Ship Token
1 Treasure Card
1 Treasure Map Card
4 Pirate Captain Cards
4 Pirate Crew Cards

From the box:

Poker Bingo consists of an exciting variety of five games using Bingo boards, chips and a deck of special size playing cards, which are all included. Each game combines the play of Bingo with a poker style.

Each player takes a Poker Bingo board and chips for markers. The dealer shuffles the cards and places them face down in front of him. He turns the top card and calls it out. The players that have the called card space on their board, cover that space with a chip. Play continues in this manner.

In Fluster, a clever cross-up word game, players race to fill two 5×5 letter grids using a shared sequence of called letter-and-number pairs. Each square in the grids is pre-numbered 1 to 25 in a random order, and when a player calls “B-12,” for instance, all players must write the letter “B” into the square labeled “12” on one of their grids—choosing left or right. Strategy comes in as players try to form the highest-scoring words across, down, or diagonally. When all squares are filled, players score their grids: each valid word earns 1 point per letter.

Whether you're building big words or blocking risky ones, Fluster mixes wordplay with tactical placement and just enough randomness to keep things lively.

Amphorae or Das Zehn Vasen Spiel is tile pickup game. A 7x7 grid is filled with 40 tiles (10 amphora pictures made up of 4 tiles each), with the 9 ‘tic tac toe’ spaces containing hostage tiles to complete the grid. You can move 2 or 3 spaces containing one right hand turn, and you can pick up the last tile you move off.

The object of the game is to pick up complete sets of amphoras.

A player can only keep two tiles in his/her hand Any tile placed in front of you that is less than a set of 3 in an amphora can be picked up by anyone who lands on a hostage tile.

So the trick is to get three tiles all in one amphora in successive turns, and lay them down immediately. These are now worth 1 point and are safe from being stolen.

A completed amphora of 4 tiles is worth 4 points.

Draw letters and place into a central area.  All players then try to make words from those letters.  Players may capture words made by other players by adding new letters from the pool to existing words. The game comes with 180 wooden letter tiles in red ink.

Wickie is a German board game for children; it is designed to promote recognition of colors and numbers.  Thematically, it is uses elements from a television series of the same name.  Players assume the roles of Wickie and his friends as they race each other to board a Viking ship.

Each player selects a character and takes four tokens.  They play their turns by first rolling the dice.  There are four lanes to reach the ship.  The squares (of which some are colored) on the lanes are printed with the faces of a die.  A player can move his or her token to the next square if one of the rolled dice matches the square's.  If the token moves onto a colored square, it can jump once to the next square of the same color.  Each character can take a short at certain points along the path.  As each square cannot accommodate more than one token, blocking is possible.  However, a token can jump across blocking pieces if the player has a die that matches the square at the opposite side of a block.

The game ends when one player gets all four of his or her tokens aboard the viking ship at the end, making him or her the winner.

Algoritmo won the first edition of the contest for unpublished games, Premio Archimede, in 1994. Quality Game published it the year after.
Each player has a little plastic computer that runs on the board to get rid of all the "output cards" he got at the beginning. Each player has a little cardboard "console" with three boxes called A, B and C. The player draws three numbers from a special deck. He arrange them on the boxes as he see fit. To move on the board, dice are not used: the little computer can advance of A + B spaces, A - B spaces or, from certain spots, of B x C spaces. Numbers can be changed in certain spaces. When the computer stops on a space with a certain letter, the player can discard the matching "output card" if he has one. When a player lands exactly on the "End" space, the one with less output cards wins.

A dark wooded is divided into 11 x 11 squares separated by grooved lines. Each player has a steel pawn and 21 steel fences.

The pawns are placed on the middle square at opposite ends of the board. Each player has to move his own pawn across the board to reach any square on the opposite side. Each turn a player can either move his pawn one square, or put a fence to close two square sides. When a player runs out of his own fences, he can only move the pawn.

Placed fences can not be removed. The only limit to placing them is that you can never totally close the enemy pawn: you have to leave a way, no matter how long, to reach its goal.

The game was been licensed to Gigamic for an international edition under the title of Quoridor. Differences are a smaller board, 4 wooden pawns (for 2/4 players), 20 wooden fences total (10 each in a 2-player game, or 5 each in a 4-player game) and the abolition of diagonal movement.

Re-implemented by:

    Quoridor

This boardgame has been published together with Warhammer: 28 periodical issues sold in the Italian newspaperkiosks. They included the game, a Warhammer Encyclopedia (by Andrea Angiolino himself) and short stories set in Warhammer world.
The boardgame is played on squared modular tiles to form dungeons and outdoor battlefields: several underground, open field and mixed scenarios are included. The issues included cardboard models of two houses and a tower, to be used in the game. Game characters depict all the main races of the Warhammer world, together with monsters and some characters from the short stories in the issues, as Felix and Gotrek.
The game uses cardboard counters, but there was on sale a box with all the lead miniatures included in the game.
The issues have been published and distributed by the Italian publisher Hobby & Work. Nexus Editrice designed and edited all the materials up to the printing stage. Games Workshop licensed the issues and provided the miniature sets.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Each player is a car builder. He can get contracts for car components (engines, electrical systems, chassis...) from all over the world, at different prices and in different currencies: dollars, ECUs and yens. Three sets of money banknotes are included in the box. 
Once the player has assembled the cars, he can sell them in Europe, America or Asia getting the price in ECUs, dollars or yens respectively. The price of the cars in each market depends on the global offer by the players. The exchange rate among the three currencies depends on their use by the players.

Hill Billy Times is an auto racing game with a twist. The object of the game is not necessarily to win, but to finish the race with the most money. It also has a set of scrap metal race cars which the game designer notes, "should be completely clean, but we cannot guarantee that they are sterile."

Components include the game board and rules (German), 30 game cards, five cars, and game money (approximately 160 chips in four colors, worth varying numbers of points).

Players bid for starting position, then draw cards to dictate movement. Each card has various hazards listed. The active player shows their card to everyone, then secretly picks a set of results and writes it down. The opponents pay insurance based on what hazards they think will be on the active player's selected option. The active player then reveals their choice and moves based on the option chosen. If the opponents did not insure themselves against the hazards that appear, the active player collects money from them.

The race ends when the racers clear one of the two rows containing stacks of bonus money out.

Solitaire ASL: WWII Tactical Warfare Solitaire Module for the Advanced Squad Leader system. Game boards not included.

Must have Advanced Squad Leader, Beyond Valor: ASL Module 1 and Yanks: ASL Module 3 to play.

Ownership of non-desert mapboards of the ASL System is recommended to make full use of this system.

260 counters
14 solitaire missions
4 Generation Cards
1 ASL Chapter S
1 ASL Chapter S divider

Complexity: Very high

Second edition adds 8 more Generation Cards and 7 more missions, and 260 more counters.

Expands:

    Advanced Squad Leader, Beyond Valor: ASL Module 1, and Yanks: ASL Module 3 (all are required)


Scenarios:

Mission 01: Cautious Advance 
Mission 02: Pockets 
Mission 03: Recon 
Mission 04: Bunker Busting 
Mission 05: Take the Highway 
Mission 06: The Fortress 
Mission 07: Block Party 
Mission 08: Patrol 
Mission 09: Enemy Offensive 
Mission 10: Hold the Line 
Mission 11: Tank Attack! 
Mission 12: Besieged 
Mission 13: The Bridge 
Mission 14: Human Wave 
Mission 15: Pursuit 
Mission 16: Airlanding 
Mission 17: River Assault! 
Mission 18: Bridgehead 
Mission 19: Escape! 
Mission 20: Amphibious Assault 
Mission 21: Beach Defense
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: (From the box) Triangle is unlike any other board game you've ever played. Yet it has one thing in common with all the classic games - it doesn't yield its secrets all at once. Played at basic level, Triangle is as enjoyable and as satisfying as draughts. It's almost as easy to play too. Except that the moves with which you capture your opponent's pieces are far more varied and interesting.
A simple representation of an athletics meeting. Lots of different events are portrayed. Players compete mainly by rolling dice.
A fast-playing simulation of American football. Cards hold offensive and defensive strategies, and stand-up card players are positioned and move about the large board executing the plays.

boardgame: The Book of Greek Myths Pop-up Board Games
description: Four separate pop-up board games and a small booklet on the myths that inspired them. The games are The Trojan Horse, Odysseus, Theseus and the Minotaur, and Daedalus and Icarus. The book includes a spinner, different stand-up pieces for each game, and a small storage pocket.

The board game that challenges you to become King of the CyberSurfers, with a CyberEmpire of Web Sites and CyberBucks.

Surf the Internet, buying, selling and trading Chat Rooms, News Desks, Computer Stores, Shopping Carts, Game Rooms, Sports Dens and more. Buy Web Sites, then upgrade them with Megabyte Chips and Gigabyte Chips to collect CyberBucks as other players hit those sites.

Increase your revenues by landing on You've Got Mail spots.  But watch out for those Virus! spots!  They can cost you CyberBucks or a feared scamming penalty or that nasty Connection Interrupted:  Go Offline!

Similar to Monopoly but selling websites instead of real estate.

A high quality trivia board game for young and adults (15 years and older), made of wood. It is innovative in both the dynamics and the questions. To obtain the correct answers the players must figure them out rather than just search in their brains. Thus, the questions are sorted by the abilities required to answer them instead of their corresponding area of knowledge.
The game is played by teams and it is conceived to minimize the frustration: most the questions can be answered by all the players, granting them the pleasure of the intellectual activity carried out successfully.

Kupido is a humorous game about love affairs. 
Each player distributes his points among six characteristics: Physical appearance, Culture, Charm, Money, Feelings, Eroticism. The first four of those scores are public the last two are secret. 
Each player also fixes the minimum scores for each characteristic of his/her ideal partner. 
The board depicts a city, divided into hexes. Players wander on it, looking for their ideal mate: a character of the opposite sex whose 4 open scores fits their secret goals. In the meantime, their own scores change: in the Gym the physical appearance increases (if there are no accidents...) while in the Fast food it can suddenly drop down!
When two characters suit each other with their 4 open characteristics, they can make a couplet. They then move together with a 4-sided die (instead that alone with a 6-sided one) until they reach a private house or a hotel. At this point they can show each other the two secret characteristics: if they don't fit they split apart. But if they fit, they win as soon as they reach the Cathedral or the City Hall.
Each hex of the map and each playing card has a humorous comment about each game effect.
To play, you do not need to have the same amount of male and female characters: you only need at least two males and two females. A rare quality of this game is that player can enter the game later or go away before the end with no problems.

If you are an Avalon Hill collector, this game is certainly one of the Holy Grails, and may well be the proverbial IT. The big one. Certainly one of the harder titles to track down. This is JZ, a.k.a. WJZ, the game created by WJZ-TV channel 13 in Baltimore, copyrighted by the Robert Goodman agency.

According to AH's Silver Jubilee guide, "WJZ was never in the Avalon Hill line; having been the only game Avalon Hill ever published as a private-label job. [p.25]" Aside from copies retained by Avalon Hill personnel, this game was distributed solely as a promotional item by WJZ-TV in Baltimore, MD (USA).

JZ bills itself as, "The TV ad man's game." Two to six players compete for high score in the form of advertising accounts and advisory fees. Players can make their accounts more valuable with fortunate advertising time purchases.

Contents for the game are as follows:


     Instruction sheet
     Game board
     2 dice
     6 pawns
     6 strips for marking each accounts' class 
     24 pink "Buy Time" cards
     12 green "Regional" account cards
     12 blue "Local" accounts
     8 brown "National" account cards 
     2 small cardboard storage inserts


JZ comes in the standard flat box form typical of AH games made in the late 1950s and early 1960s.

The "Buy Time" cards, of course, are an exercise in shameless self-promotion by WJZ's advertising department -- both for the station and its parent company, Westinghouse. Some of the cards are now a bit dated, with references to buying time on the Buddy Deane Show and the Steve Allen Show.

Luck plays a far greater role than skill, as JZ is a simple roll-and-move game. Each player hopes to land on favorable spaces or draw cards that will allow them to land on and upgrade advertising accounts, going along the board until they bail out at "Retirement" or reach "THE BITTER END". The person with the most valuable advertising portfolio at the end of the game is the winner.

A 3 dimensional board game where Spiderman swings from a web suspended between 4 towers above a rotatable disc simulating a manhole cover.

Bad guy discs are located on points adjacent to points on the manhole discs.

Movement is controlled by a single deck of cards. players take turns to draw a card and follow instructions. after use the card is discarded, when all the cards have been used the game is over.

Cards instruct to move yourself, a bad guy disc swing spidey or rotate the central disc. the object is to collect as many bad guys as possible before the card deck runs out.

the winner is the player with the most bad guys.

Spiderman swings around and seemingly at random chooses a bad guy to be collected.

Your quest is about to begin. Think you'd make a good secret agent?

Put your spy tactics to the test. In SpyQuest(TM), you'll watch one of three action-packed espionage adventure movies on your own VCR. Each of these half-hour long Hollywood-quality motion pictures was produced exclusively for SpyQuest and bridges the gap between live-action adventure and you, the player. Next, you'll be traveling the world uncovering clues to a secret location. Sound easy? Not so fast. You'll have to decipher the configuration of a key, tooth by tooth. You'll be interrogated about your knowledge of the film as you compete against other agents on the same mission. They'll use road blocks, naval mines, spy scopes, and sabotage kits against you to reach the location first. Of course, you can use the same tactics on them, so anything goes! Be the first agent to uncover the secret location, travel there, and survive a triple interrogation and you're proven secret agent material. With five sets of interrogation questions and 64 different secret location possibilities, you'll have a new and different adventure every time you play. Good luck and may the best spy win!

—description from the back of the box

With the increasing public concern over the growing number of endangered species and the inhumane treatment of animals, FAMILY GAMES has developed a line of board games, card games and puzzles in order to enlighten children of all ages to the animals’ predicament.

ENDANGERED is a realistic and fast paced game which makes participants aware of the plight of endangered species as they travel the world searching for reasons and remedies to aid their chosen animal.

BrickQuest is a game of small-scale fantasy combat similar to HeroQuest, Warhammer Quest, or MageKnight Dungeons. It requires at least two players to play and can be played easily by four to six players.

One player takes on the role of the BrickMaster. His job is to create, using construction blocks, a variety of dungeon rooms in which the game will take place, and creatures to inhabit them. Ideally these rooms will be modular so they can be added to the table one at a time as players enter them or get sight of them.

The remaining players will play heroic characters who enter the dungeon in pursuit of gold and glory.

Since BrickQuest is a boardgame, it uses a higher level of abstraction than roleplaying games. Rather than trying to capture all the nuances of fantasy combat, it uses simplified rules for quick and balanced play.

You are a squirrel! Yes, you are and you need to collect nuts. But the other squirrels want them too. The tiles with the nuts have a different value. How to get them? You have cards for your movement. Each player has the same cards. The player with the most points of nuts wins. You have to bluff and manage with your cards. This is a fun and bluff game.

Be an animal at the zoo!
